**Marriage**, also called **matrimony** or **wedlock**, is a culturally and often legally recognised union between people called [spouses](/wiki/Spouse "Spouse"). It establishes rights and obligations between them, as well as between them and their children (if any), and between them and their [in-laws](/wiki/Affinity_(law) "Affinity (law)").[[1]](#cite_note-1) It is nearly a [cultural universal](/wiki/Cultural_universal "Cultural universal"),[[2]](#cite_note-2) but the definition of marriage varies between [cultures](/wiki/Culture "Culture") and [religions](/wiki/Religion "Religion"), and over time. Typically, it is an institution in which interpersonal relationships, usually sexual, are acknowledged or sanctioned. In some cultures, marriage is recommended or considered to be [compulsory before pursuing sexual activity](/wiki/Premarital_sex "Premarital sex"), and sexual intercourse with others aside from one's spouse may be considered immoral or even illegal.[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3) A marriage ceremony is called a [wedding](/wiki/Wedding "Wedding"), while a private marriage is sometimes called an [elopement](/wiki/Elopement "Elopement").

Marriage takes many forms, depending on cultural and religious values of the people involved and the cultural expectations of the society in which they are living.[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3) Marriage may be recognized by a state, an organization, a religious authority, a tribal group, a local community, or peers. It is often viewed as a [legal contract](/wiki/Legal_contract "Legal contract"). Around the world, there has been a general trend towards ensuring [equal rights for women](/wiki/Women%27s_rights "Women's rights") in marriages with men, and greater tolerance toward non-traditional couples. Debates persist regarding the legal status of married women, leniency towards violence within marriage, customs such as [dowry](/wiki/Dowry "Dowry") and [bride price](/wiki/Bride_price "Bride price"), [marriageable age](/wiki/Marriageable_age "Marriageable age"), and criminalization of premarital and [extramarital sex](/wiki/Extramarital_sex "Extramarital sex").

## Etymology

The word *marriage* appeared around 1300 and is borrowed from [Old French](/wiki/Old_French "Old French") *mariage* (12th century), ultimately tracing to the [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") *maritātus* 'married', [past participle](/wiki/Past_participle "Past participle") of *maritāre* 'to marry'.[[4]](#cite_note-4) The adjective **marītus, -a, -um** 'matrimonial, nuptial' could also be used, through [nominalization](/wiki/Nominalization "Nominalization"), in the masculine form as a noun for 'husband' and in the feminine form for 'wife'.[[5]](#cite_note-OED_marriage-5) The related word *matrimony* is borrowed from the Old French word **matremoine**, which appears around 1300 CE and is in turn ultimately a [learned borrowing](/wiki/Loanword#Romance_languages "Loanword") from Latin **mātrimōnium**, which is derived from **māter** '[mother](/wiki/Mother "Mother")' with the suffix *-mōnium* for an action, state, or condition.[[6]](#cite_note-Etymology-6)

## Definitions

[Anthropologists](/wiki/Anthropologist "Anthropologist") have proposed several competing definitions of marriage in an attempt to encompass the wide variety of marital practices observed across cultures.[[7]](#cite_note-Bell-7) Even within [Western culture](/wiki/Western_culture "Western culture"), "definitions of marriage have careened from one extreme to another and everywhere in between" (as Evan Gerstmann has put it).[[8]](#cite_note-8)

### Relation recognized by custom or law

In *[The History of Human Marriage](/wiki/The_History_of_Human_Marriage "The History of Human Marriage")* (1891), [Edvard Westermarck](/wiki/Edvard_Westermarck "Edvard Westermarck") defined marriage as "a more or less durable connection between male and female lasting beyond the mere act of propagation till after the birth of the offspring."[[9]](#cite_note-9) In *The Future of Marriage in Western Civilization* (1936), he rejected his earlier definition, instead provisionally defining marriage as "a relation of one or more men to one or more women that is recognized by custom or law".[[10]](#cite_note-10)

### Legitimacy of offspring

The anthropological handbook *Notes and Queries* (1951) defined marriage as "a union between a man and a woman such that children born to the woman are the recognized legitimate offspring of both partners."[[11]](#cite_note-Notes-11) In recognition of a practice by the [Nuer people](/wiki/Nuer_people "Nuer people") of Sudan allowing women to act as a husband in certain circumstances (the [ghost marriage](/wiki/Ghost_marriage_(Sudanese) "Ghost marriage (Sudanese)")), [Kathleen Gough](/wiki/Kathleen_Gough "Kathleen Gough") suggested modifying this to "a woman and one or more other persons."[[12]](#cite_note-Gough-12)

In an analysis of marriage among the [Nayar](/wiki/Nair "Nair"), a [polyandrous](/wiki/Polyandrous "Polyandrous") society in India, Gough found that the conventional "husband" role was divided between a non-resident "social father" of the woman's children and her lovers. None of these men had legal rights to the woman's child. This forced Gough to disregard sexual access as a key element of marriage and to define it in terms of legitimacy of offspring alone.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

Economic anthropologist [Duran Bell](/wiki/Duran_Bell "Duran Bell") has criticized the legitimacy-based definition on the basis that some societies do not require marriage for legitimacy. He argued that a legitimacy-based definition of marriage is circular in societies where illegitimacy has no other legal or social implications for a child other than the mother being unmarried.[[7]](#cite_note-Bell-7)

### Collection of rights

[Edmund Leach](/wiki/Edmund_Leach "Edmund Leach") criticized Gough's definition for being too restrictive in terms of recognized legitimate offspring and suggested that marriage be viewed in terms of the different types of rights it serves to establish. In a 1955 article in *[Man](/wiki/Journal_of_the_Royal_Anthropological_Institute "Journal of the Royal Anthropological Institute")*, Leach argued that no one definition of marriage applied to all cultures. He offered a list of ten rights associated with marriage, including sexual monopoly and rights with respect to children, with specific rights differing across cultures. Those rights, according to Leach, included:

1. "To establish a legal father of a woman's children.
2. To establish a legal mother of a man's children.
3. To give the husband a monopoly in the wife's sexuality.
4. To give the wife a monopoly in the husband's sexuality.
5. To give the husband partial or monopolistic rights to the wife's domestic and other labor services.
6. To give the wife partial or monopolistic rights to the husband's domestic and other labor services.
7. To give the husband partial or total control over property belonging or potentially accruing to the wife.
8. To give the wife partial or total control over property belonging or potentially accruing to the husband.
9. To establish a joint fund of property – a partnership – for the benefit of the children of the marriage.
10. To establish a socially significant 'relationship of affinity' between the husband and his wife's brothers."[[14]](#cite_note-14)

### Right of sexual access

See also: [Marital debt](/wiki/Marital_debt "Marital debt") and [Restitution of conjugal rights](/wiki/Restitution_of_conjugal_rights "Restitution of conjugal rights")

In a 1997 article in *[Current Anthropology](/wiki/Current_Anthropology "Current Anthropology")*, [Duran Bell](/wiki/Duran_Bell "Duran Bell") describes marriage as "a relationship between one or more men (male or female) in severalty to one or more women that provides those men with a demand-right of sexual access within a domestic group and identifies women who bear the obligation of yielding to the demands of those specific men." In referring to "men in severalty", Bell is referring to corporate kin groups such as lineages which, in having paid bride price, retain a right in a woman's offspring even if her husband (a lineage member) deceases ([Levirate marriage](/wiki/Levirate_marriage "Levirate marriage")). In referring to "men (male or female)", Bell is referring to women within the lineage who may stand in as the "social fathers" of the wife's children born of other lovers. (See Nuer "[ghost marriage](/wiki/Ghost_marriage_(Sudanese) "Ghost marriage (Sudanese)")".)[[7]](#cite_note-Bell-7)

## Types

Main article: [Types of marriages](/wiki/Types_of_marriages "Types of marriages")

### Monogamy

[![The marriage of Inanna and Dumuzid](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/c/cc/Marriage_of_Inanna_and_Dumuzi.png)](/wiki/File:Marriage_of_Inanna_and_Dumuzi.png)

Ancient [Sumerian](/wiki/Sumer "Sumer") depiction of the marriage of [Inanna](/wiki/Inanna "Inanna") and [Dumuzid](/wiki/Dumuzid_the_Shepherd "Dumuzid the Shepherd")[[15]](#cite_note-15)

Main article: [Monogamy](/wiki/Monogamy "Monogamy")

Monogamy is a form of marriage in which an individual has only one spouse during their lifetime or at any one time. Monogamy may or may not indicate that the spouses have pledged not to have sexual relationships outside of their marriage.[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/Hindu_wedding_couple.jpg/250px-Hindu_wedding_couple.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hindu_wedding_couple.jpg)

[Indian Hindu wedding](/wiki/Hindu_wedding "Hindu wedding") with the bride and groom in traditional dress.

Anthropologist [Jack Goody](/wiki/Jack_Goody "Jack Goody")'s comparative study of marriage around the world utilizing the [Ethnographic Atlas](/wiki/Human_Relations_Area_Files "Human Relations Area Files") found a strong correlation between intensive plough agriculture, [dowry](/wiki/Dowry "Dowry") and monogamy. This pattern was found in a broad swath of Eurasian societies from Japan to Ireland. The majority of Sub-Saharan African societies that practice extensive hoe agriculture, in contrast, show a correlation between "[bride price](/wiki/Bride_price "Bride price")" and polygamy.[[16]](#cite_note-Goody_1976_7-16) A further study drawing on the [Ethnographic Atlas](/wiki/Ethnographic_Atlas "Ethnographic Atlas") showed a statistical correlation between increasing size of the society, the belief in "high gods" to support human morality, and monogamy.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

In the countries which do not permit polygamy, a person who marries in one of those countries a person while still being lawfully married to another commits the crime of [bigamy](/wiki/Bigamy "Bigamy"). In all cases, the second marriage is considered legally null and void. Besides the second and subsequent marriages being void, the bigamist is also liable to other penalties, which also vary between jurisdictions.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Serial monogamy

Governments that support monogamy may allow easy divorce. In a number of [Western countries](/wiki/Western_Countries "Western Countries"), divorce rates approach 50%. Those who remarry do so usually no more than three times.[[18]](#cite_note-18) Divorce and remarriage can thus result in "serial monogamy", i.e. having multiple marriages but only one legal spouse at a time. This can be interpreted as a form of plural mating, as are those societies dominated by female-headed families in the [Caribbean](/wiki/Caribbean "Caribbean"), [Mauritius](/wiki/Mauritius "Mauritius") and [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil") where there is frequent rotation of unmarried partners. In all, these account for 16 to 24% of the "monogamous" category.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

Serial monogamy creates a new kind of relative, the "ex-". The "ex-wife", for example, may remain an active part of her "ex-husband's" or "ex-wife's" life, as they may be tied together by transfers of resources (alimony, child support), or shared child custody.

### Polygamy

Main article: [Polygamy](/wiki/Polygamy "Polygamy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Kwong_Sue_Duk_with_his_three_wives_and_fourteen_children%2C_Cairns%2C_1904_%289623512597%29.jpg/250px-Kwong_Sue_Duk_with_his_three_wives_and_fourteen_children%2C_Cairns%2C_1904_%289623512597%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kwong_Sue_Duk_with_his_three_wives_and_fourteen_children,_Cairns,_1904_(9623512597).jpg)

Chinese immigrant with his three wives and fourteen children, [Cairns](/wiki/Cairns "Cairns"), 1904

Polygamy is a marriage which includes more than two spouses.[[20]](#cite_note-Zeitzen-20) When a man is married to more than one wife at a time, the relationship is called [polygyny](/wiki/Polygyny "Polygyny"), and there is no marriage bond between the wives; and when a woman is married to more than one husband at a time, it is called [polyandry](/wiki/Polyandry "Polyandry"), and there is no marriage bond between the husbands. If a marriage includes multiple husbands or wives, it can be called [group marriage](/wiki/Group_marriage "Group marriage").[[20]](#cite_note-Zeitzen-20)

A molecular genetic study of global human genetic diversity argued that sexual polygyny was typical of human reproductive patterns until the shift to sedentary farming communities approximately 10,000 to 5,000 years ago in Europe and Asia, and more recently in Africa and the Americas.[[21]](#cite_note-21) As noted above, Anthropologist [Jack Goody](/wiki/Jack_Goody "Jack Goody")'s comparative study of marriage around the world utilizing the [Ethnographic Atlas](/wiki/Human_Relations_Area_Files "Human Relations Area Files") found that the majority of Sub-Saharan African societies that practice extensive hoe agriculture show a correlation between "[Bride price](/wiki/Bride_price "Bride price")" and polygamy.[[16]](#cite_note-Goody_1976_7-16) A survey of other cross-cultural samples has confirmed that the absence of the plough was the only predictor of polygamy, although other factors such as high male mortality in warfare (in non-state societies) and pathogen stress (in state societies) had some impact.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

Marriages are classified according to the number of legal spouses an individual has. The suffix "-gamy" refers specifically to the number of spouses, as in [bi-gamy](/wiki/Bigamy "Bigamy") (two spouses, generally illegal in most nations), and poly-gamy (more than one spouse).

Societies show variable acceptance of polygamy as a cultural ideal and practice. According to the [Ethnographic Atlas](/wiki/Human_Relations_Area_Files "Human Relations Area Files"), of 1,231 societies noted, 186 were monogamous; 453 had occasional polygyny; 588 had more frequent polygyny, and 4 had polyandry.[[23]](#cite_note-Atlas-23) However, as Miriam Zeitzen writes, social tolerance for polygamy is different from the practice of polygamy, since it requires wealth to establish multiple households for multiple wives. The actual prevalence of polygamy in a tolerant society may be low, with the majority of people practicing monogamous marriage. Tracking the occurrence of polygamy is further complicated in jurisdictions where it has been banned, but continues to be practiced (*de facto polygamy*).[[24]](#cite_note-Zeitzen_2008_5-24)

Zeitzen also notes that Western perceptions of African society and marriage patterns are biased by "contradictory concerns of nostalgia for traditional African culture versus critique of polygamy as oppressive to women or detrimental to development."[[24]](#cite_note-Zeitzen_2008_5-24) Polygamy has been condemned as being a form of human rights abuse, with concerns arising over domestic abuse, forced marriage, and neglect.[[by whom?](/wiki/Weasel_word "Weasel word")] The vast majority of countries do not permit polygamy.

#### Polygyny

Main article: [Polygyny](/wiki/Polygyny "Polygyny")

See also: [Concubinage](/wiki/Concubinage "Concubinage")

Polygyny usually grants wives equal status, although the husband may have personal preferences. One type of de facto polygyny is [concubinage](/wiki/Concubinage "Concubinage"), where only one woman gets a wife's rights and status, while other women remain legal house mistresses.

Although a society may be classified as polygynous, not all marriages in it necessarily are; monogamous marriages may in fact predominate. It is to this flexibility that Anthropologist [Robin Fox](/wiki/Robin_Fox_(anthropologist) "Robin Fox (anthropologist)") attributes its success as a social support system:

> This has often meant – given the imbalance in the sex ratios, the higher male infant mortality, the shorter life span of males, the loss of males in wartime, etc. – that often women were left without financial support from husbands. To correct this condition, females had to be killed at birth, remain single, become prostitutes, or be siphoned off into celibate religious orders. Polygynous systems have the advantage that they can promise, as did the Mormons, a home and family for every woman.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

Nonetheless, polygyny is a gender issue which offers men asymmetrical benefits. In some cases, there is a large age discrepancy (as much as a generation) between a man and his youngest wife, compounding the power differential between the two. Tensions not only exist *between* genders, but also *within* genders; senior and junior men compete for wives, and senior and junior wives in the same household may experience radically different life conditions, and internal hierarchy. Several studies have suggested that the wive's relationship with other women, including co-wives and husband's female kin, are more critical relationships than that with her husband for her productive, reproductive and personal achievement.[[26]](#cite_note-26) In some societies, the co-wives are relatives, usually sisters, a practice called *sororal polygyny*; the pre-existing relationship between the co-wives is thought to decrease potential tensions within the marriage.[[27]](#cite_note-Zeitzen_2008_9-27)

Fox argues that "the major difference between polygyny and monogamy could be stated thus: while plural mating occurs in both systems, under polygyny several unions may be recognized as being legal marriages while under monogamy only one of the unions is so recognized. Often, however, it is difficult to draw a hard and fast line between the two."[[28]](#cite_note-28)

As polygamy in Africa is increasingly subject to legal limitations, a variant form of *de facto* (as opposed to legal or *de jure*) polygyny is being practiced in urban centers. Although it does not involve multiple (now illegal) formal marriages, the domestic and personal arrangements follow old polygynous patterns. The de facto form of polygyny is found in other parts of the world as well (including some Mormon sects and Muslim families in the United States).[[29]](#cite_note-29)
In some societies such as the [Lovedu](/wiki/Lobedu_people "Lobedu people") of South Africa, or the [Nuer](/wiki/Nuer_people "Nuer people") of the Sudan, aristocratic women may become female 'husbands.' In the Lovedu case, this female husband may take a number of polygamous wives. This is not a lesbian relationship, but a means of legitimately expanding a royal lineage by attaching these wives' children to it. The relationships are considered polygynous, not polyandrous, because the female husband is in fact assuming masculine gendered political roles.[[27]](#cite_note-Zeitzen_2008_9-27)

Religious groups have differing views on the legitimacy of [polygyny](/wiki/Polygyny "Polygyny"). It is allowed in [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") and [Confucianism](/wiki/Confucianism "Confucianism"). [Judaism](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism") and [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") have mentioned practices involving polygyny in the past, however, outright religious acceptance of such practices was not addressed until its rejection in later passages. They do explicitly prohibit polygyny today.

#### Polyandry

Main articles: [Polyandry](/wiki/Polyandry "Polyandry"), [Polyandry in Tibet](/wiki/Polyandry_in_Tibet "Polyandry in Tibet"), and [Polyandry in India](/wiki/Polyandry_in_India "Polyandry in India")

[Polyandry](/wiki/Polyandry "Polyandry") is notably more rare than polygyny, though less rare than the figure commonly cited in the *Ethnographic Atlas* (1980) which listed only those polyandrous societies found in the Himalayan Mountains. More recent studies have found 53 societies outside the 28 found in the Himalayans which practice polyandry.[[30]](#cite_note-30) It is most common in egalitarian societies marked by high male mortality or male absenteeism. It is associated with *partible paternity*, the cultural belief that a child can have more than one father.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

The explanation for polyandry in the Himalayan Mountains is related to the scarcity of land; the marriage of all brothers in a family to the same wife (*fraternal polyandry*) allows family land to remain intact and undivided. If every brother married separately and had children, family land would be split into unsustainable small plots. In Europe, this was prevented through the social practice of impartible inheritance (the dis-inheriting of most siblings, some of whom went on to become celibate monks and priests).[[32]](#cite_note-32)

#### Plural marriage

[Group marriage](/wiki/Group_marriage "Group marriage") (also known as *multi-lateral marriage*) is a form of [polyamory](/wiki/Polyamory "Polyamory") in which more than two persons form a [family](/wiki/Family "Family") unit, with all the members of the group marriage being considered to be married to all the other members of the group marriage, and all members of the marriage share [parental](/wiki/Parent "Parent") responsibility for any children arising from the marriage.[[33]](#cite_note-murdock-1949-definition-33) No country legally condones group marriages, neither under the law nor as a common law marriage, but historically it has been practiced by some cultures of Polynesia, Asia, Papua New Guinea and the Americas – as well as in some [intentional communities](/wiki/Intentional_community "Intentional community") and alternative subcultures such as the [Oneida Perfectionists](/wiki/Oneida_Community "Oneida Community") in up-state New York. Of the 250 societies reported by the American anthropologist [George Murdock](/wiki/George_Murdock "George Murdock") in 1949, only the [Kaingang](/wiki/Kaingang "Kaingang") of Brazil had any group marriages at all.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

### Child marriage

Main article: [Child marriage](/wiki/Child_marriage "Child marriage")

A child marriage is a marriage where one or both spouses are under the age of 18.[[35]](#cite_note-Child_Marriage-35)[[36]](#cite_note-36) It is related to child [betrothal](/wiki/Engagement#Betrothal "Engagement") and [teenage pregnancy](/wiki/Teenage_pregnancy "Teenage pregnancy").

Child marriage was common throughout history. In 1880 in the United States, the minimum age of consent for marriage ranged from 7 to 12 years old.[[37]](#cite_note-gmu-aoc-37) Today it is condemned by international human rights organizations.[[38]](#cite_note-bbc.co.uk-38)[[39]](#cite_note-who.int-39) Child marriages are often arranged between the families of the future bride and groom, sometimes as soon as the girl is born.[[38]](#cite_note-bbc.co.uk-38) However, in the late 1800s in England and the United States, [feminist](/wiki/Feminism "Feminism") activists began calling for raised age of consent laws, which was eventually handled in the 1920s, having been raised to 16–18.[[40]](#cite_note-encyclopedia-aoc-40) Still, in 2017, over half of the 50 United States have no explicit minimum age to marry and several states set the age as low as 14.[[41]](#cite_note-tahirih-41)

Child marriages can also occur in the context of [bride kidnapping](/wiki/Bride_kidnapping "Bride kidnapping").[[38]](#cite_note-bbc.co.uk-38)

While child marriage is observed for both boys and girls, the overwhelming majority of child spouses are girls.[[42]](#cite_note-42) In many cases, only one marriage-partner is a child, usually the female, due to the importance placed upon female [virginity](/wiki/Virginity "Virginity").[[38]](#cite_note-bbc.co.uk-38) Causes of child marriage include [poverty](/wiki/Poverty "Poverty"), [bride price](/wiki/Bride_price "Bride price"), [dowry](/wiki/Dowry "Dowry"), laws that allow child marriages, [religious](/wiki/Religion "Religion") and [social pressures](/wiki/Peer_pressure "Peer pressure"), regional customs, fear of remaining unmarried, and perceived inability of women to work for money.

Today, child marriages are widespread in parts of the world; being most common in [South Asia](/wiki/South_Asia "South Asia") and [sub-Saharan Africa](/wiki/Sub-Saharan_Africa "Sub-Saharan Africa"), with more than half of the girls in some countries in those regions being married before 18.[[38]](#cite_note-bbc.co.uk-38) The incidence of child marriage has been falling in most parts of the world. In developed countries, child marriage is outlawed or restricted.

Girls who marry before 18 are at greater risk of becoming victims of [domestic violence](/wiki/Domestic_violence "Domestic violence"), than those who marry later, especially when they are married to a much older man.[[39]](#cite_note-who.int-39)

### Same-sex and third-gender marriages

Main articles: [Same-sex marriage](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage "Same-sex marriage") and [History of same-sex unions](/wiki/History_of_same-sex_unions "History of same-sex unions")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/Terryandmarkwedding.jpg/250px-Terryandmarkwedding.jpg)](/wiki/File:Terryandmarkwedding.jpg)

A same-sex couple exchanging wedding vows in a [Unitarian Universalist](/wiki/Unitarian_Universalism "Unitarian Universalism") Fellowship

Several kinds of same-sex marriages have been documented in Indigenous and lineage-based cultures. In the Americas, [We'wha](/wiki/We%27wha "We'wha") ([Zuni](/wiki/Zuni_people "Zuni people")), was a *[lhamana](/wiki/Lhamana "Lhamana")* (male individuals who, at least some of the time, dress and live in the roles usually filled by women in that culture); a respected artist, We'wha served as an emissary of the Zuni to Washington, where he met President [Grover Cleveland](/wiki/Grover_Cleveland "Grover Cleveland").[[43]](#cite_note-Stevenson37-43) We'wha had at least one husband who was generally recognized as such.[[44]](#cite_note-44)

While it is a relatively new practice to grant same-sex couples the same form of legal marital recognition as commonly granted to mixed-sex couples, there is some history of recorded same-sex unions around the world.[[45]](#cite_note-same_sex_marriage_a09-45) [Ancient Greek same-sex relationships](/wiki/Homosexuality_in_ancient_Greece "Homosexuality in ancient Greece") were like modern companionate marriages, unlike their different-sex marriages in which the spouses had few emotional ties, and the husband had freedom to engage in outside sexual liaisons. The [Codex Theodosianus](/wiki/Codex_Theodosianus "Codex Theodosianus") (*C. Th.* 9.7.3) issued in 438 [CE](/wiki/Common_Era "Common Era") imposed severe penalties or death on same-sex relationships,[[46]](#cite_note-46) but the exact intent of the law and its relation to social practice is unclear, as only a few examples of same-sex relationships in that culture exist.[[47]](#cite_note-47) Same-sex unions were celebrated in some regions of China, such as [Fujian](/wiki/Fujian "Fujian").[[48]](#cite_note-48) Possibly the earliest documented same-sex wedding in [Latin Christendom](/wiki/Latin_Church "Latin Church") occurred in [Rome, Italy](/wiki/Rome "Rome"), at the [San Giovanni a Porta Latina](/wiki/San_Giovanni_a_Porta_Latina "San Giovanni a Porta Latina") basilica in 1581.[[49]](#cite_note-49)

### Temporary marriages

Main articles: [Islamic marital jurisprudence](/wiki/Islamic_marital_jurisprudence "Islamic marital jurisprudence"), [Islamic marital practices](/wiki/Islamic_marital_practices "Islamic marital practices"), and [Marriage in Islam](/wiki/Marriage_in_Islam "Marriage in Islam")

Further information: [History of concubinage in the Muslim world](/wiki/History_of_concubinage_in_the_Muslim_world "History of concubinage in the Muslim world"), [Polygamy in Islam](/wiki/Polygamy_in_Islam "Polygamy in Islam"), and [Prostitution in the Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Prostitution_in_the_Ottoman_Empire "Prostitution in the Ottoman Empire")

Examples of temporary marriage include the practices of "walking marriage" among the matrilineal [Mosuo people](/wiki/Mosuo_people "Mosuo people") of [China](/wiki/China "China"),[[50]](#cite_note-50) of [handfasting](/wiki/Handfasting "Handfasting") among some [Germanic peoples](/wiki/Germanic_peoples "Germanic peoples") during the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"),[[51]](#cite_note-51) and fixed-term marriages in the [Muslim world](/wiki/Muslim_world "Muslim world").[[52]](#cite_note-El-Alami_2023-52)[[53]](#cite_note-Wyers_2017-53)[[54]](#cite_note-El-Alami_1995-54)

Historically, [pre-Islamic Arab societies](/wiki/Religion_in_pre-Islamic_Arabia "Religion in pre-Islamic Arabia") practiced a form of temporary marriage that carries on today in the practice of *[nikāh mutʿah](/wiki/Nikah_mut%27ah "Nikah mut'ah")* ("temporary gratification" or "temporary gift"),[[52]](#cite_note-El-Alami_2023-52)[[54]](#cite_note-El-Alami_1995-54) a fixed-term marriage contract of varying duration[[53]](#cite_note-Wyers_2017-53) (otherwise known as *[sigheh](/wiki/Nikah_mut%27ah "Nikah mut'ah")* in [Iran](/wiki/Iran "Iran") and *mutʿah* in [Iraq](/wiki/Iraq "Iraq")).[[53]](#cite_note-Wyers_2017-53) The [Islamic prophet](/wiki/Islamic_prophet "Islamic prophet") [Muḥammad](/wiki/Muhammad "Muhammad") explicitly sanctioned [his followers](/wiki/Companions_of_the_Prophet "Companions of the Prophet") from enganging into the practice of temporary marriages, which he considered to be a form of *[zināʾ](/wiki/Zina "Zina")* ("[adultery](/wiki/Adultery "Adultery")" and/or "[fornication](/wiki/Fornication "Fornication")"),[[55]](#cite_note-EoQ-Quran-55)[[56]](#cite_note-EncyclopediaofIslam-56) an umbrella term which specifically refers to unlawful sexual intercourse in the [Qurʾān](/wiki/Quran "Quran") and [Islamic law](/wiki/Sharia "Sharia").[[55]](#cite_note-EoQ-Quran-55)[[56]](#cite_note-EncyclopediaofIslam-56) The practice of *[zināʾ](/wiki/Zina "Zina")*, which covers any form of illicit sexual relations in the [Qurʾān](/wiki/Quran "Quran") and [Islamic jurisprudence](/wiki/Fiqh "Fiqh"),[[55]](#cite_note-EoQ-Quran-55)[[56]](#cite_note-EncyclopediaofIslam-56) and can provide a legitimizing cover for [non-marital sex](/wiki/Adultery "Adultery"), [prostitution](/wiki/Prostitution "Prostitution"), [pimping](/wiki/Pimping "Pimping"), and [sex workers](/wiki/Sex_work "Sex work"),[[57]](#cite_note-57) is commonly conflated with [prostitution](/wiki/Prostitution "Prostitution") under the legal statutes of [Muslim-majority countries](/wiki/Muslim-majority_countries "Muslim-majority countries") and is officially prohibited as illegal in all of them, with the only exception of [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey") where [prostitution is legalized](/wiki/Prostitution_in_Turkey "Prostitution in Turkey").[[58]](#cite_note-58)

The same forms of temporary marriage have been used in [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt"), [Lebanon](/wiki/Lebanon "Lebanon"), and [Iran](/wiki/Iran "Iran") to make the donation of a human ova legal for [in vitro fertilisation](/wiki/In_vitro_fertilisation "In vitro fertilisation"); a woman cannot, however, use this kind of marriage to obtain a [sperm donation](/wiki/Sperm_donation "Sperm donation").[[59]](#cite_note-59) In recent times, [societal controversies related to the practice of *nikāh mutʿah*](/wiki/Nikah_mut%27ah#Western_views "Nikah mut'ah") in [Sunnī-majority](/wiki/Sunni_Islam "Sunni Islam") [Muslim countries](/wiki/Islam_by_country "Islam by country") have resulted in the practice being confined mostly to [Shīʿa Muslim communities](/wiki/Shia_Islam "Shia Islam").[[53]](#cite_note-Wyers_2017-53)

### Cohabitation

See also: [Cohabitation](/wiki/Cohabitation "Cohabitation") and [Common-law marriage](/wiki/Common-law_marriage "Common-law marriage")

In some jurisdictions [cohabitation](/wiki/Cohabitation "Cohabitation"), in certain circumstances, may constitute a [common-law marriage](/wiki/Common-law_marriage "Common-law marriage"), an [unregistered partnership](/wiki/Unregistered_cohabitation "Unregistered cohabitation"), or otherwise provide the unmarried partners with various rights and responsibilities; and in some countries, the laws recognize cohabitation in lieu of institutional marriage for taxation and social security benefits. This is the case, for example, in Australia.[[60]](#cite_note-60) Cohabitation may be an option pursued as a form of resistance to traditional institutionalized marriage. However, in this context, some nations reserve the right to define the relationship as marital, or otherwise to regulate the relation, even if the relation has not been registered with the state or a religious institution.[[61]](#cite_note-61)

Conversely, institutionalized marriages may not involve cohabitation. In some cases, couples living together do not wish to be recognized as married. This may occur because pension or alimony rights are adversely affected; because of taxation considerations; because of immigration issues, or for other reasons. Such marriages have also been increasingly common in [Beijing](/wiki/Beijing "Beijing"). Guo Jianmei, director of the center for women's studies at Beijing University, told a [Newsday](/wiki/Newsday "Newsday") correspondent, "Walking marriages reflect sweeping changes in Chinese society." A "walking marriage" refers to a type of temporary marriage formed by the [Mosuo](/wiki/Mosuo "Mosuo") of China, in which male partners live elsewhere and make nightly visits.[[62]](#cite_note-62) A similar arrangement in [Saudi Arabia](/wiki/Saudi_Arabia "Saudi Arabia"), called [misyar marriage](/wiki/Nikah_Misyar "Nikah Misyar"), also involves the husband and wife living separately but meeting regularly.[[63]](#cite_note-63)

## Partner selection

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9e/18281018_Wife_Wanted_-_Royal_Cornwall_Gazette%2C_Falmouth_Packet_and_Plymouth_Journal.jpg/500px-18281018_Wife_Wanted_-_Royal_Cornwall_Gazette%2C_Falmouth_Packet_and_Plymouth_Journal.jpg)](/wiki/File:18281018_Wife_Wanted_-_Royal_Cornwall_Gazette,_Falmouth_Packet_and_Plymouth_Journal.jpg)

In an 1828 "Wife Wanted" advertisement, an Englishman claiming a "great taste for building" pledges to apply a prospective wife's [dowry](/wiki/Dowry "Dowry")-like £1000+ to build property that will be "settled on her for life".[[64]](#cite_note-CornwallGazette_18281018-64)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/Louis_Magrath_King_and_Rinchen_Lhamo_wedding_photo.jpg/250px-Louis_Magrath_King_and_Rinchen_Lhamo_wedding_photo.jpg)](/wiki/File:Louis_Magrath_King_and_Rinchen_Lhamo_wedding_photo.jpg)

The wedding of [Rinchen Lhamo](/wiki/Rinchen_Lhamo "Rinchen Lhamo"), a [Tibetan](/wiki/Tibetan_people "Tibetan people") woman, and [Louis King](/wiki/Louis_Magrath_King "Louis Magrath King"), an [Englishman](/wiki/English_people "English people")

There is wide cross-cultural variation in the social rules governing the selection of a partner for marriage. There is variation in the degree to which partner selection is an individual decision by the partners or a collective decision by the partners' kin groups, and there is variation in the rules regulating which partners are valid choices.

The United Nations World Fertility Report of 2003 reports that 89% of all people get married before age forty-nine.[[65]](#cite_note-UnitedNations,_2004-65) The percent of women and men who marry before age forty-nine drops to nearly 50% in some nations and reaches near 100% in other nations.[[66]](#cite_note-UnitedNations,_2000-66)

In other cultures with less strict rules governing the groups from which a partner can be chosen the selection of a marriage partner may involve either the couple going through a selection process of [courtship](/wiki/Courtship "Courtship") or the marriage may be [arranged](/wiki/Arranged_marriage "Arranged marriage") by the couple's parents or an outside party, a [matchmaker](/wiki/Matchmaking "Matchmaking").

### Age difference

Main article: [Age disparity in sexual relationships](/wiki/Age_disparity_in_sexual_relationships "Age disparity in sexual relationships")

See also: [Social stigma](/wiki/Social_stigma "Social stigma")

Some people want to marry a person that is older or younger than they. This may impact marital stability[[67]](#cite_note-67) and partners with more than a 10-year age gap tend to experience social disapproval.[[68]](#cite_note-68) In addition, older women (older than 35) have increased health risks when getting pregnant.[[69]](#cite_note-69)

### Social status and wealth

Some people want to marry a person with higher or lower status than them. Others want to marry people who have similar status. In many societies, women marry men who are of higher social status.[[70]](#cite_note-70) There are marriages where each party has sought a partner of similar status.

Some persons also wish to engage in transactional relationship for money (thus a type of marriage of convenience). Such people are sometimes referred to as [gold diggers](/wiki/Gold_digger "Gold digger"). [Separate property systems](/wiki/Matrimonial_regime "Matrimonial regime") can however be used to prevent property of being passed on to partners after divorce or death.

Higher income men are more likely to marry and less likely to divorce. High income women are more likely to divorce.[[71]](#cite_note-71)

### Incest taboo, exogamy and endogamy

Further information: [Prohibited degree of kinship](/wiki/Prohibited_degree_of_kinship "Prohibited degree of kinship"), [Cousin marriage](/wiki/Cousin_marriage "Cousin marriage"), [Affinity (Catholic canon law)](/wiki/Affinity_(Catholic_canon_law) "Affinity (Catholic canon law)"), and [Avunculate marriage](/wiki/Avunculate_marriage "Avunculate marriage")

Societies have often placed restrictions on marriage to relatives, though the degree of prohibited relationship varies widely. Marriages between parents and children, or between full siblings, with few exceptions,[[72]](#cite_note-72)[[73]](#cite_note-73)[[74]](#cite_note-74)[[75]](#cite_note-75)[[76]](#cite_note-76)[[77]](#cite_note-77)[[78]](#cite_note-78) have been considered [incest](/wiki/Incest "Incest") and forbidden. However, [marriages between more distant relatives](/wiki/Cousin_marriage "Cousin marriage") have been much more common, with one estimate being that 80% of all marriages in history have been between second cousins or closer.[[79]](#cite_note-79) This proportion has fallen dramatically, but still, more than 10% of all marriages are believed to be between people who are second cousins or more closely related.[[80]](#cite_note-80) In the United States, such marriages are now highly stigmatized, and laws ban most or all first-cousin marriage in 30 states. In South Korea, historically it was illegal to marry someone with the same last name and same ancestral line.[[81]](#cite_note-81)

An [Avunculate marriage](/wiki/Avunculate_marriage "Avunculate marriage") is a marriage that occurs between an uncle and his niece or between an aunt and her nephew. Such marriages are illegal in most countries due to incest restrictions. However, a small number of countries have legalized it, including [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina "Argentina"), [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"), [Austria](/wiki/Austria "Austria"), [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia"),[[82]](#cite_note-82) and [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia").[[83]](#cite_note-83)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/2/2f/Mahrams_Chart.png/500px-Mahrams_Chart.png)](/wiki/File:Mahrams_Chart.png)

Family chart showing relatives who, in Islamic Sharia law, would be considered *mahrim* (or *maharem*): unmarriageable kin with whom [sexual intercourse](/wiki/Sexual_intercourse "Sexual intercourse") would be considered [incestuous](/wiki/Incest "Incest")

In various societies, the choice of partner is often limited to suitable persons from specific social groups. In some societies the rule is that a partner is selected from an individual's own social group – [endogamy](/wiki/Endogamy "Endogamy"), this is often the case in class- and caste-based societies. But in other societies a partner must be chosen from a different group than one's own – [exogamy](/wiki/Exogamy "Exogamy"), this may be the case in societies practicing [totemic](/wiki/Totem "Totem") religion where society is divided into several exogamous totemic clans, such as most [Aboriginal Australian](/wiki/Aboriginal_Australians "Aboriginal Australians") societies. In other societies a person is expected to marry their [cross-cousin](/wiki/Parallel_and_cross_cousins "Parallel and cross cousins"), a woman must marry her father's sister's son and a man must marry his mother's brother's daughter – this is often the case if either a society has a rule of tracing kinship exclusively through patrilineal or matrilineal descent groups as among the [Akan people](/wiki/Akan_people "Akan people") of West Africa.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Another kind of marriage selection is the [levirate marriage](/wiki/Levirate_marriage "Levirate marriage") in which widows are obligated to marry their husband's brother, mostly found in societies where kinship is based on endogamous clan groups.

Religion has commonly weighed in on the matter of which relatives, if any, are allowed to marry. Relations may be by [consanguinity](/wiki/Consanguinity "Consanguinity") or [affinity](/wiki/Affinity_(law) "Affinity (law)"), meaning by blood or by marriage. On the marriage of cousins, [Catholic](/wiki/Catholicism "Catholicism") policy has evolved from initial acceptance, through a long period of general prohibition, to the contemporary requirement for a dispensation.[[84]](#cite_note-84) [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") has always allowed it, while [Hindu texts](/wiki/Hindu_texts "Hindu texts") vary widely.[[85]](#cite_note-85)[[86]](#cite_note-indiasocialstructure-86)

### Prescriptive marriage

Main article: [Arranged marriage](/wiki/Arranged_marriage "Arranged marriage")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/Lodewijk_XIV-Marriage.jpg/250px-Lodewijk_XIV-Marriage.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lodewijk_XIV-Marriage.jpg)

An arranged marriage between [Louis XIV of France](/wiki/Louis_XIV_of_France "Louis XIV of France") and [Maria Theresa of Spain](/wiki/Maria_Theresa_of_Spain "Maria Theresa of Spain")

In a wide array of lineage-based societies with a [classificatory kinship system](/wiki/Kinship "Kinship"), potential spouses are sought from a specific class of relative as determined by a prescriptive marriage rule. This rule may be expressed by anthropologists using a "descriptive" kinship term, such as a "man's mother's brother's daughter" (also known as a "cross-cousin"). Such descriptive rules mask the participant's perspective: a man should marry a woman from his mother's lineage. Within the society's kinship terminology, such relatives are usually indicated by a specific term which sets them apart as potentially marriageable. [Pierre Bourdieu](/wiki/Pierre_Bourdieu "Pierre Bourdieu") notes, however, that very few marriages ever follow the rule, and that when they do so, it is for "practical kinship" reasons such as the preservation of family property, rather than the "official kinship" ideology.[[87]](#cite_note-87)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/Minangkabau_wedding_2.jpg/250px-Minangkabau_wedding_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Minangkabau_wedding_2.jpg)

Indonesian wedding

Insofar as regular marriages following prescriptive rules occur, lineages are linked together in fixed relationships; these ties between lineages may form political alliances in kinship dominated societies.[[88]](#cite_note-88) French [structural](/wiki/Structural_functionalism "Structural functionalism") anthropologist [Claude Lévi-Strauss](/wiki/Claude_L%C3%A9vi-Strauss "Claude Lévi-Strauss") developed [alliance theory](/wiki/Alliance_theory "Alliance theory") to account for the "elementary" kinship structures created by the limited number of prescriptive marriage rules possible.[[89]](#cite_note-89)

A pragmatic (or 'arranged') marriage is made easier by formal procedures of family or group politics. A responsible authority sets up or encourages the marriage; they may, indeed, engage a professional [matchmaker](/wiki/Matchmaking "Matchmaking") to find a suitable spouse for an unmarried person. The authority figure could be parents, family, a religious official, or a group consensus.[[90]](#cite_note-90)

### Forced marriage

Main article: [Forced marriage](/wiki/Forced_marriage "Forced marriage")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bd/Oskar_Shmerling._Free_love_%28Forced_marriage%29._Molla_Nasreddin.jpg/250px-Oskar_Shmerling._Free_love_%28Forced_marriage%29._Molla_Nasreddin.jpg)](/wiki/File:Oskar_Shmerling._Free_love_(Forced_marriage)._Molla_Nasreddin.jpg)

Criticism about the [Azeri](/wiki/Azerbaijani_people "Azerbaijani people") society tradition from domestic violence to the social and political participation of women in the community

A forced marriage is a marriage in which one or both of the parties is married against their will. Forced marriages continue to be practiced in parts of the world, especially in [South Asia](/wiki/South_Asia "South Asia") and [Africa](/wiki/Africa "Africa"). The line between forced marriage and consensual marriage may become blurred, because the social norms of these cultures dictate that one should never oppose the desire of one's parents/relatives in regard to the choice of a spouse; in such cultures, it is not necessary for violence, threats, intimidation etc. to occur, the person simply "consents" to the marriage even if they do not want it, out of the implied social pressure and duty. The customs of [bride price](/wiki/Bride_price "Bride price") and [dowry](/wiki/Dowry "Dowry"), that exist in parts of the world, can lead to [bride buying](/wiki/Bride_buying "Bride buying") and selling people into marriage.[[91]](#cite_note-91)[[92]](#cite_note-92)

In some societies, ranging from [Central Asia](/wiki/Central_Asia "Central Asia") to the [Caucasus](/wiki/Caucasus "Caucasus") to Africa, the custom of [bride kidnapping](/wiki/Bride_kidnapping "Bride kidnapping") still exists, in which a woman is captured by a man and his friends. Sometimes this covers an [elopement](/wiki/Elopement "Elopement"), but sometimes it depends on [sexual violence](/wiki/Sexual_violence "Sexual violence"). In previous times, *[raptio](/wiki/Raptio "Raptio")* was a larger-scale version of this, with groups of women captured by groups of men, sometimes in war; the most famous example is [The Rape of the Sabine Women](/wiki/The_Rape_of_the_Sabine_Women "The Rape of the Sabine Women"), which provided the first citizens of Rome with their wives.

Other marriage partners are more or less imposed on an individual. For example, [widow inheritance](/wiki/Widow_inheritance "Widow inheritance") provides a widow with another man from her late husband's brothers.

In rural areas of India, [child marriage](/wiki/Child_marriage "Child marriage") is practiced, with parents often arranging the wedding, sometimes even before the child is born.[[93]](#cite_note-93) This practice was made illegal under the [Child Marriage Restraint Act](/wiki/Child_Marriage_Restraint_Act "Child Marriage Restraint Act") of 1929.

## Economic considerations

See also: [Economics of marriage](/wiki/Economics_of_marriage "Economics of marriage") and [Family economics](/wiki/Family_economics "Family economics")

The financial aspects of marriage vary between cultures and have changed over time.

In some cultures, dowries and bride wealth continue to be required today. In both cases, the financial arrangements are usually made between the groom (or his family) and the bride's family; with the bride often not being involved in the negotiations, and often not having a choice in whether to participate in the marriage.

In [Early modern Britain](/wiki/Early_modern_Britain "Early modern Britain"), the social status of the couple was supposed to be equal. After the marriage, all the property (called "fortune") and expected inheritances of the wife belonged to the husband.

### Dowry

A [dowry](/wiki/Dowry "Dowry") is

> a process whereby parental property is distributed to a daughter at her marriage (i.e. *inter vivos*) rather than at the holder's death (*mortis causa*)… A dowry establishes some variety of conjugal fund, the nature of which may vary widely. This fund ensures her support (or endowment) in widowhood and eventually goes to provide for her sons and daughters.[[94]](#cite_note-94)

In some cultures, especially in countries such as [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey"), [India](/wiki/India "India"), [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh"), [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan"), [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lanka "Sri Lanka"), [Morocco](/wiki/Morocco "Morocco"), [Nepal](/wiki/Nepal "Nepal"), dowries continue to be expected. In India, thousands of dowry-related deaths have taken place on yearly basis,[[95]](#cite_note-95)[[96]](#cite_note-96) to counter this problem, several jurisdictions have enacted laws restricting or banning dowry (see [Dowry law in India](/wiki/Dowry_system_in_India#Dowry_Prohibition_Act,_1961 "Dowry system in India")). In Nepal, dowry was made illegal in 2009.[[97]](#cite_note-97) Some authors believe that the giving and receiving of dowry reflects the status and even the effort to climb high in social hierarchy.[[98]](#cite_note-98)

### Dower

Main article: [Dower](/wiki/Dower "Dower")

Direct Dowry contrasts with [bride wealth](/wiki/Bride_price "Bride price"), which is paid by the groom or his family to the bride's parents, and with indirect dowry (or [dower](/wiki/Dower "Dower")), which is property given to the bride herself by the groom at the time of marriage and which remains under her ownership and control.[[99]](#cite_note-99)

In the Jewish tradition, the rabbis in ancient times insisted on the marriage couple entering into a [prenuptial agreement](/wiki/Prenuptial_agreement "Prenuptial agreement"), called a *[ketubah](/wiki/Ketubah "Ketubah")*. Besides other things, the *ketubah* provided for an amount to be paid by the husband in the event of a [divorce](/wiki/Get_(divorce_document) "Get (divorce document)") or his estate in the event of his death. This amount was a replacement of the biblical [dower](/wiki/Dower "Dower") or [bride price](/wiki/Bride_price "Bride price"), which was payable at the time of the marriage by the groom to the father of the bride, because many young prospective husbands could not raise the bride price at the time when they would normally be expected to marry. It is the predecessor to the wife's present-day entitlement to [maintenance](/wiki/Alimony "Alimony") in the event of the breakup of marriage, and family maintenance in the event of the husband not providing adequately for the wife in his [will](/wiki/Will_and_testament "Will and testament"). Another function performed by the *ketubah* amount was to provide a disincentive for the husband contemplating divorcing his wife.

[Morning gifts](/wiki/Dower "Dower"), which might also be arranged by the bride's father rather than the bride, are given to the bride herself; the name derives from the Germanic tribal custom of giving them the morning after the wedding night. She might have control of this morning gift during the lifetime of her husband, but is entitled to it when widowed. If the amount of her inheritance is settled by law rather than agreement, it may be called [dower](/wiki/Dower "Dower"). Depending on legal systems and the exact arrangement, she may not be entitled to dispose of it after her death, and may lose the property if she remarries. Morning gifts were preserved for centuries in [morganatic marriage](/wiki/Morganatic_marriage "Morganatic marriage"), a union where the wife's inferior social status was held to prohibit her children from inheriting a noble's titles or estates. In this case, the morning gift would support the wife and children. Another legal provision for widowhood was [jointure](/wiki/Jointure "Jointure"), in which property, often land, would be held in joint tenancy, so that it would automatically go to the widow on her husband's death.

Islamic tradition has similar practices. A '[mahr](/wiki/Mahr "Mahr")', either immediate or deferred, is the woman's portion of the groom's wealth (divorce) or estate (death). These amounts are usually set on the basis of the groom's own and family wealth and incomes, but in some parts these are set very high so as to provide a disincentive for the groom exercising the divorce, or the husband's family 'inheriting' a large portion of the estate, especially if there are no male offspring from the marriage. In some countries, including Iran, the mahr or alimony can amount to more than a man can ever hope to earn, sometimes up to US$1,000,000 (4000 official Iranian gold coins). If the husband cannot pay the mahr, either in case of a divorce or on demand, according to the current laws in Iran, he will have to pay it by installments. Failure to pay the [mahr](/wiki/Mahr "Mahr") might even lead to imprisonment.[[100]](#cite_note-100)

### Bridewealth

Main article: [Bride price](/wiki/Bride_price "Bride price")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cd/Thai_Bride_Price_2008.jpg/250px-Thai_Bride_Price_2008.jpg)](/wiki/File:Thai_Bride_Price_2008.jpg)

Traditional, formal presentation of the bridewealth (also known as "sin sot") at an engagement ceremony in [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand")

Bridewealth is a common practice in parts of [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia") ([Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand"), [Cambodia](/wiki/Cambodia "Cambodia")), parts of [Central Asia](/wiki/Central_Asia "Central Asia"), and in much of [sub-Saharan Africa](/wiki/Sub-Saharan_Africa "Sub-Saharan Africa"). It is also known as brideprice although this has fallen in disfavor as it implies the purchase of the bride. Bridewealth is the amount of [money](/wiki/Money "Money") or [property](/wiki/Property "Property") or wealth paid by the groom or his family to the parents of a woman upon the marriage of their daughter to the groom. In [anthropological](/wiki/Anthropology "Anthropology") literature, bride price has often been explained as payment made to compensate the bride's family for the loss of her labor and fertility. In some cases, bridewealth is a means by which the groom's family's ties to the children of the union are recognized.

### Taxation

In some countries a married person or couple benefits from various taxation advantages not available to a single person. For example, spouses may be allowed to average their combined [incomes](/wiki/Income_tax "Income tax"). This is advantageous to a married couple with disparate incomes. To compensate for this, countries may provide a higher [tax bracket](/wiki/Tax_bracket "Tax bracket") for the averaged income of a married couple. While income averaging might still benefit a married couple with a stay-at-home spouse, such averaging would cause a married couple with roughly equal personal incomes to pay more total tax than they would as two single persons. In the United States, this is called the [marriage penalty](/wiki/Marriage_penalty "Marriage penalty").[[101]](#cite_note-101)

When the rates applied by the tax code are not based income averaging, but rather on the *sum* of individuals' incomes, higher rates will usually apply to each individual in a two-earner households in a [progressive tax](/wiki/Progressive_tax "Progressive tax") systems. This is most often the case with high-income taxpayers and is another situation called a marriage penalty.[[102]](#cite_note-102)

Conversely, when progressive tax is levied on the individual with no consideration for the partnership, dual-income couples fare much better than single-income couples with similar household incomes. The effect can be increased when the welfare system treats the same income as a shared income thereby denying welfare access to the non-earning spouse. Such systems apply in Australia and Canada, for example.[[103]](#cite_note-103)

### Divorce

Divorce is the other end of marriage that couples often do not go into marriage hoping to do. Although the past years rates of divorce have been decreasing as a result of individuals marrying late and rise in education, there is still a common trend of it in the lower and less educated social class. The CDC reports that of the 2,236,496 marriages 787,251 couples ended in a divorce.[[104]](#cite_note-104) This means that 35 percent of marriages ended in divorce with an overall industrial worth of $50 billion yearly.[[105]](#cite_note-Economics_of_marriage_:2-105) The significance of this data in relation to the topic of the economy of marriage can be explained by a report given by Jay Zagorsky, the writer of Marriage and Divorce's Impact on Wealth. This report revealed that over the course of time it is more profitable to stay married than to have a divorce. According to the article by Zagorsky, “Married respondents experience per person net worth increases of 77 percent over single respondents. [While] divorced respondents’ wealth starts falling four years before divorce and they experience an average wealth drop of 77 percent.”[[106]](#cite_note-106) Mary Corcoran study done in 1994 looked at the same families while they were married and when they separated. The study shows that the family income that once averaged $43,600 (married) declined to averaging $25,300.[[107]](#cite_note-Economics_of_marriage_:3-107)[[108]](#cite_note-108) One of the causes of this contrast is that, married couples are more likely to invest because of their commitment to each other hence the reason why many are homeowners compared to their single counterparts. A second reason for this is that married couples share things together unlike separated or single individuals. As a married household there ought to be no need for multiple of the same home appliances, maybe cars, and they can divide the labour and expenses of raising children/housework.[[105]](#cite_note-Economics_of_marriage_:2-105) A second facet to consider when looking at divorce in relations to the economic of marriage is that of children. A study done by Julia Heath and B. F. Kiker reveal that compared to any other population single-mother headed households are susceptible to poverty. Secondary data collected for this study reports that, “changes in family structure precede poverty spells in over 99 percent of the white single-mother families…and in almost 97 percent of black families."[[109]](#cite_note-109) Another study related to this data by saying, “75 percent of all women who apply for welfare benefits do so because of a disrupted marriage or a disrupted relationship in which they live with a male outside of marriage”.[[107]](#cite_note-Economics_of_marriage_:3-107) The parent that took the children under their possession experiences an overall decline in his/her income.

## Post-marital residence

In many Western cultures, marriage usually leads to the formation of a new household comprising the married couple, with the married couple living together in the same home, often sharing the same bed, but in some other cultures this is not the tradition.[[110]](#cite_note-110) Among the [Minangkabau](/wiki/Minangkabau_people "Minangkabau people") of [West Sumatra](/wiki/West_Sumatra "West Sumatra"), residency after marriage is [matrilocal](/wiki/Matrilocal_residence "Matrilocal residence"), with the husband moving into the household of his wife's mother.[[111]](#cite_note-111) Residency after marriage can also be [patrilocal](/wiki/Patrilocal_residence "Patrilocal residence") or [avunculocal](/wiki/Avunculate#Avunculocal_societies "Avunculate"). In these cases, married couples may not form an independent household, but remain part of an extended family household.

Early theories explaining the determinants of postmarital residence[[112]](#cite_note-112) connected it with the sexual division of labor. However, to date, [cross-cultural](/wiki/Cross-cultural "Cross-cultural") tests of this [hypothesis](/wiki/Hypothesis "Hypothesis") using worldwide samples have failed to find any significant relationship between these two variables. However, [Korotayev](/wiki/Andrey_Korotayev "Andrey Korotayev")'s tests show that the female contribution to subsistence does correlate significantly with matrilocal residence in general. However, this correlation is masked by a general polygyny factor.

Although, in different-sex marriages, an increase in the female contribution to subsistence tends to lead to matrilocal residence, it also tends simultaneously to lead to general non-sororal [polygyny](/wiki/Polygyny "Polygyny") which effectively destroys [matrilocality](/wiki/Matrilocal_residence "Matrilocal residence"). If this polygyny factor is controlled (e.g., through a multiple [regression](/wiki/Regression_analysis "Regression analysis") model), division of labor turns out to be a significant predictor of postmarital residence. Thus, Murdock's hypotheses regarding the relationships between the sexual division of labor and postmarital residence were basically correct, though[[113]](#cite_note-113) the actual relationships between those two groups of variables are more complicated than he expected.[[114]](#cite_note-114)[[115]](#cite_note-115)

There has been a trend toward the [neolocal residence](/wiki/Neolocal_residence "Neolocal residence") in western societies.[[116]](#cite_note-116)

Main article: [Marriage law](/wiki/Marriage_law "Marriage law")

Marriage laws refer to the legal requirements which determine the validity of a marriage, which vary considerably between countries. Article 16 of the [Universal Declaration of Human Rights](/wiki/Universal_Declaration_of_Human_Rights "Universal Declaration of Human Rights") declares that:

> 1. Men and women of full age, without any limitation due to race, nationality or religion, have the right to marry and to found a family. They are entitled to equal rights as to marriage, during marriage and at its dissolution.
>
> 2. Marriage shall be entered into only with the free and full consent of the intending spouses.
> 3. The family is the natural and fundamental group unit of society and is entitled to protection by society and the State.

— [Universal Declaration of Human Rights, Article 16](https://www.un.org/en/about-us/universal-declaration-of-human-rights)

### Rights and obligations

See also: [Family rights](/wiki/Family_rights "Family rights"), [Matrimonial regime](/wiki/Matrimonial_regime "Matrimonial regime"), and [Prenuptial agreement](/wiki/Prenuptial_agreement "Prenuptial agreement")

A marriage bestows rights and obligations on the married parties, and sometimes on [relatives](/wiki/Kinship "Kinship") as well, being the sole mechanism for the creation of [affinal ties](/wiki/Affinity_(law) "Affinity (law)") (in-laws). These may include, depending on jurisdiction:

* Giving one spouse or his/her family control over the other spouse's sexual services, labor, and property.
* Giving one spouse responsibility for the other's debts.
* Giving one spouse visitation rights when the other is incarcerated or hospitalized.
* Giving one spouse control over the other's affairs when the other is incapacitated.
* Establishing the second [legal guardian](/wiki/Legal_guardian "Legal guardian") of a parent's child.
* Establishing a [joint fund of property](/wiki/Community_property "Community property") for the benefit of children.
* Establishing a relationship between the families of the spouses.

These rights and obligations vary considerably between societies, and between groups within society.[[117]](#cite_note-117) These might include arranged marriages, family obligations, the legal establishment of a [nuclear family](/wiki/Nuclear_family "Nuclear family") unit, the legal protection of children and public declaration of [commitment](/wiki/Promise "Promise").[[118]](#cite_note-118)[[119]](#cite_note-119)

### Property regime

In many countries today, each marriage partner has the choice of keeping his or her property [separate](/wiki/Community_property "Community property") or combining properties. In the latter case, called [community property](/wiki/Community_property "Community property"), when the marriage ends by divorce each owns half. In lieu of a [will](/wiki/Will_and_testament "Will and testament") or [trust](/wiki/Trust_law "Trust law"), property owned by the deceased generally is inherited by the surviving spouse.

In some legal systems, the partners in a marriage are "jointly liable" for the debts of the marriage. This has a basis in a traditional legal notion called the "Doctrine of Necessities" whereby, in a heterosexual marriage, a husband was responsible to provide necessary things for his wife. Where this is the case, one partner may be sued to collect a debt for which they did not expressly contract. Critics of this practice note that debt collection agencies can abuse this by claiming an unreasonably wide range of debts to be expenses of the marriage. The cost of defense and the burden of proof is then placed on the non-contracting party to prove that the expense is not a debt of the family. The respective maintenance obligations, both during and eventually after a marriage, are regulated in most [jurisdictions](/wiki/Jurisdiction "Jurisdiction"); [alimony](/wiki/Alimony "Alimony") is one such method.

### Restrictions

Marriage is an institution that is historically filled with restrictions. From age, to race, to social status, to [consanguinity](/wiki/Consanguinity "Consanguinity"), to gender, restrictions are placed on marriage by society for reasons of benefiting the children, passing on healthy genes, maintaining cultural values, or because of [prejudice](/wiki/Prejudice "Prejudice") and [fear](/wiki/Fear "Fear"). Almost all cultures that recognize marriage also recognize [adultery](/wiki/Adultery "Adultery") as a violation of the terms of marriage.[[120]](#cite_note-Encyclopædia_Britannica_adultery-120)

#### Age

Most jurisdictions set a [minimum age for marriage](/wiki/Marriageable_age "Marriageable age"); that is, a person must attain a certain age to be legally allowed to marry. This age may depend on circumstances, for instance exceptions from the general rule may be permitted if the parents of a young person express their consent and/or if a court decides that said marriage is in the best interest of the young person (often this applies in cases where a girl is pregnant). Although most age restrictions are in place in order to prevent children from being forced into marriages, especially to much older partners – marriages which can have negative education and health related consequences, and lead to [child sexual abuse](/wiki/Child_sexual_abuse "Child sexual abuse") and other forms of violence[[121]](#cite_note-121) – such [child marriages](/wiki/Child_marriage "Child marriage") remain common in parts of the world. According to the UN, child marriages are most common in rural [sub-Saharan Africa](/wiki/Sub-Saharan_Africa "Sub-Saharan Africa") and [South Asia](/wiki/South_Asia "South Asia"). The ten countries with the highest rates of child marriage are: [Niger](/wiki/Niger "Niger") (75%), Chad, Central African Republic, Bangladesh, Guinea, Mozambique, Mali, Burkina Faso, South Sudan, and Malawi.[[122]](#cite_note-122)

#### Kinship

Main article: [Cousin marriage](/wiki/Cousin_marriage "Cousin marriage")

See also: [Coefficient of relationship](/wiki/Coefficient_of_relationship "Coefficient of relationship")

To prohibit incest and eugenic reasons, marriage laws have set restrictions for relatives to marry. Direct blood relatives are usually prohibited to marry, while for branch line relatives, laws are wary.[[123]](#cite_note-123)[[124]](#cite_note-124)

Kinship relations through marriage is also called "affinity", relationships that arise in one's group of origin, can also be called one's descent group. Some cultures in kinship relationships may be considered to extend out to those who they have economic or political relationships with; or other forms of social connections. Within some cultures they may lead you back to gods[[125]](#cite_note-125) or animal ancestors (totems). This can be conceived of on a more or less literal basis.

#### Race

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/20/US_miscegenation.svg/500px-US_miscegenation.svg.png)](/wiki/File:US_miscegenation.svg)

U.S States, by the date of repeal of anti-miscegenation laws:

No laws passed

Repealed before 1887

Repealed between 1948 and 1967

Overturned on 12 June 1967

Main article: [Interracial marriage](/wiki/Interracial_marriage "Interracial marriage")

Laws banning "race-mixing" were enforced in certain North American jurisdictions from 1691[[126]](#cite_note-Frank_W_Sweet-126) until 1967, in [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") (the [Nuremberg Laws](/wiki/Nuremberg_Laws "Nuremberg Laws")) from 1935 until 1945, and in South Africa during most part of the [apartheid](/wiki/Apartheid "Apartheid") era (1949–1985). All these laws primarily banned marriage between persons of different racially or ethnically defined groups, which was termed "amalgamation" or "miscegenation" in the U.S. The laws in Nazi Germany and many of the U.S. states, as well as South Africa, also banned sexual relations between such individuals.

In the United States, laws in some but not all of the states prohibited the marriage of whites and blacks, and in many states also the intermarriage of whites with [Native Americans](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States") or [Asians](/wiki/Asian_Americans "Asian Americans").[[127]](#cite_note-127) In the U.S., such laws were known as [anti-miscegenation laws](/wiki/Anti-miscegenation_laws "Anti-miscegenation laws"). From 1913 until 1948, 30 out of the then 48 states enforced such laws.[[128]](#cite_note-128) Although an "Anti-Miscegenation Amendment" to the [United States Constitution](/wiki/United_States_Constitution "United States Constitution") was proposed in 1871, in 1912–1913, and in 1928,[[129]](#cite_note-129)[[130]](#cite_note-130) no nationwide law against racially mixed marriages was ever enacted. In 1967, the [Supreme Court of the United States](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_States "Supreme Court of the United States") unanimously ruled in *[Loving v. Virginia](/wiki/Loving_v._Virginia "Loving v. Virginia")* that anti-miscegenation laws are [unconstitutional](/wiki/Constitutionality "Constitutionality"). With this ruling, these laws were no longer in effect in the remaining 16 states that still had them.

The Nazi ban on interracial marriage and interracial sex was enacted in September 1935 as part of the [Nuremberg Laws](/wiki/Nuremberg_Laws "Nuremberg Laws"), the *Gesetz zum Schutze des deutschen Blutes und der deutschen Ehre* (The Law for the Protection of German Blood and German Honour). The Nuremberg Laws classified [Jews](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism") as a race and forbade marriage and extramarital sexual relations at first with people of Jewish descent, but was later ended to the "Gypsies, Negroes or their bastard offspring" and people of "German or related blood".[[131]](#cite_note-Burleigh1991-131) Such relations were marked as *[Rassenschande](/wiki/Rassenschande "Rassenschande")* (lit. 'race-disgrace') and could be punished by imprisonment (usually followed by deportation to a concentration camp) and even by death.

In South Africa, the [Prohibition of Mixed Marriages Act, 1949](/wiki/Prohibition_of_Mixed_Marriages_Act,_1949 "Prohibition of Mixed Marriages Act, 1949") prohibited marriage between persons of different races, and the [Immorality Act](/wiki/Immorality_Act "Immorality Act") of 1950 made [sexual relations with a person of a different race](/wiki/Miscegenation "Miscegenation") a [crime](/wiki/Crime "Crime").
