the United Kingdom"),[[c]](#cite_note-uk-134) the [United States](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage_in_the_United_States "Same-sex marriage in the United States"),[[d]](#cite_note-usa-135) and [Uruguay](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage_in_Uruguay "Same-sex marriage in Uruguay"). [Israel](/wiki/Recognition_of_same-sex_unions_in_Israel "Recognition of same-sex unions in Israel") recognizes same-sex marriages entered into abroad as full marriages.

The introduction of same-sex marriage has varied by jurisdiction, being variously accomplished through legislative change to [marriage law](/wiki/Marriage_law "Marriage law"), a court ruling based on constitutional guarantees of equality, or by direct popular vote (via [ballot initiative](/wiki/Ballot_initiative "Ballot initiative") or [referendum](/wiki/Referendum "Referendum")). The recognition of same-sex marriage is considered to be a [human right](/wiki/Human_rights "Human rights") and a [civil right](/wiki/Civil_rights "Civil rights") as well as a political, social, and religious issue.[[132]](#cite_note-136) The most prominent supporters of same-sex marriage are human rights and civil rights organizations as well as the medical and scientific communities, while the most prominent opponents are religious groups. Various faith communities around the world support same-sex marriage, while many religious groups oppose it. [Polls consistently show](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage#Public_opinion "Same-sex marriage") continually rising support for the recognition of same-sex marriage in all developed democracies and in some developing democracies.[[133]](#cite_note-137)

The establishment of recognition in law for the marriages of same-sex couples is one of the most prominent objectives of the [LGBT rights](/wiki/LGBT_rights "LGBT rights") movement.

#### Number of spouses

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/63/Legality_of_polygamy.svg/500px-Legality_of_polygamy.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Legality_of_polygamy.svg)

Polygamy is legal

Polygamy is legal only for Muslims

Polygamy is illegal, but practice is not criminalised

Polygamy is illegal and practice criminalised

Legal status unknown

* In India, Malaysia, Philippines and Singapore polygamy is only legal for [Muslims](/wiki/Muslim "Muslim").
* In Nigeria and South Africa, polygamous marriages under customary law and for Muslims are legally recognized.
* In Mauritius, polygamous unions have no legal recognition. Muslim men may, however, "marry" up to four women, but they do not have the legal status of wives.

Main article: [Legality of polygamy](/wiki/Legality_of_polygamy "Legality of polygamy")

Polygyny is widely practiced in mostly [Muslim](/wiki/Muslim "Muslim") and African countries.[[134]](#cite_note-138)[[135]](#cite_note-139) In the Middle Eastern region, Israel, Turkey and [Tunisia](/wiki/Polygamy_in_Tunisia "Polygamy in Tunisia") are notable exceptions.[[136]](#cite_note-140)

In most other jurisdictions, polygamy is illegal. For example, In the United States, polygamy is illegal in all [50 states](/wiki/List_of_states_and_territories_of_the_United_States "List of states and territories of the United States").[[137]](#cite_note-quietly-141)

In the late-19th century, citizens of the self-governing territory of what is present-day [Utah](/wiki/Utah "Utah") were forced by the United States federal government to abandon the practice of [polygamy](/wiki/Polygamy "Polygamy") through the vigorous enforcement of several [Acts of Congress](/wiki/Act_of_Congress "Act of Congress"), and eventually complied. [The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints](/wiki/The_Church_of_Jesus_Christ_of_Latter-day_Saints "The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints") formally abolished the practice in 1890, in a document labeled '[The Manifesto](/wiki/1890_Manifesto "1890 Manifesto")' (see [Latter Day Saint polygamy in the late-19th century](/wiki/Latter_Day_Saint_polygamy_in_the_late-19th_century "Latter Day Saint polygamy in the late-19th century")).[[138]](#cite_note-142) Among [American Muslims](/wiki/Islam_in_the_United_States "Islam in the United States"), a small minority of around 50,000 to 100,000 people are estimated to live in families with a husband maintaining an illegal polygamous relationship.[[137]](#cite_note-quietly-141)

Several countries such as India and Sri Lanka,[[139]](#cite_note-143) permit only their Islamic citizens to practice polygamy. Some Indians have converted to [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") in order to bypass such legal restrictions.[[140]](#cite_note-144) Predominantly Christian nations usually do not allow [polygamous unions](/wiki/Polygamy "Polygamy"), with a handful of exceptions being the [Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Polygamy_in_the_Republic_of_the_Congo "Polygamy in the Republic of the Congo"), Uganda, and [Zambia](/wiki/Polygamy_in_Zambia "Polygamy in Zambia").

### State recognition

When a marriage is performed and carried out by a government institution in accordance with the [marriage laws](/wiki/Marriage_law "Marriage law") of the jurisdiction, without religious content, it is a [civil marriage](/wiki/Civil_marriage "Civil marriage"). Civil marriage recognizes and creates the rights and obligations intrinsic to matrimony in the eyes of the state. Some countries do not recognize locally performed religious marriage on its own, and require a separate civil marriage for official purposes. Conversely, civil marriage does not exist in some countries governed by a [religious legal system](/wiki/Theocracy "Theocracy"), such as [Saudi Arabia](/wiki/Saudi_Arabia "Saudi Arabia"), where marriages contracted abroad might not be recognized if they were contracted contrary to Saudi interpretations of [Islamic religious law](/wiki/Sharia "Sharia"). In countries governed by a [mixed secular-religious legal system](/wiki/Millet_system "Millet system"), such as [Lebanon](/wiki/Lebanon "Lebanon") and [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel"), locally performed civil marriage does not exist within the country, which prevents interfaith and various other marriages that contradict religious laws from being entered into in the country; however, civil marriages performed abroad may be recognized by the state even if they conflict with religious laws. For example, in the case of recognition of [marriage in Israel](/wiki/Marriage_in_Israel "Marriage in Israel"), this includes recognition of not only interfaith civil marriages performed abroad, but also overseas same-sex civil marriages.

In various jurisdictions, a civil marriage may take place as part of the religious marriage ceremony, although they are theoretically distinct. Some jurisdictions allow civil marriages in circumstances which are notably not allowed by particular religions, such as [same-sex marriages](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage "Same-sex marriage") or [civil unions](/wiki/Civil_union "Civil union"). Churches may have less strict limits than the civil jurisdictions.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] It is possible for two people to be recognized as married by a religious or other institution, but not by the state, and hence without the legal rights and obligations of marriage; or to have a civil marriage deemed invalid and sinful by a religion. Similarly, a couple may remain married in religious eyes after a civil divorce.

Most sovereign states and other jurisdictions limit legally recognized marriage to [opposite-sex](/wiki/Heterosexuality "Heterosexuality") couples and a diminishing number of these permit [polygyny marriage](/wiki/Polygyny "Polygyny"), [polyandry marriage](/wiki/Polyandry "Polyandry"), [group marriage](/wiki/Group_marriage "Group marriage"), [coverture marriage](/wiki/Coverture "Coverture"), [arranged marriages](/wiki/Arranged_marriage "Arranged marriage"), [forced marriages](/wiki/Forced_marriage "Forced marriage"), [child marriages](/wiki/Child_marriage "Child marriage"), [cousin marriages](/wiki/Cousin_marriage "Cousin marriage"), [sibling marriages](/wiki/Sibling_marriage "Sibling marriage"), [teenage marriages](/wiki/Teenage_marriage "Teenage marriage"), [avunculate marriages](/wiki/Avunculate_marriage "Avunculate marriage"), [incestuous marriages](/wiki/Incest "Incest"), and [bestiality marriages](/wiki/Human-animal_marriage "Human-animal marriage"). In modern times, a growing number of countries, primarily developed democracies, have lifted bans on, and have established legal recognition for, [equal rights for women](/wiki/Women%E2%80%99s_rights "Women’s rights") and the marriages of [interethnic](/wiki/Interethnic_marriage "Interethnic marriage"), [interracial](/wiki/Interracial_marriage "Interracial marriage"), [interfaith](/wiki/Interfaith_marriage "Interfaith marriage"), [interdenominational](/wiki/Interdenominational_marriage "Interdenominational marriage"), [interclass](/wiki/Social_class "Social class"), [intercommunity](/wiki/Inter-caste_marriage "Inter-caste marriage"), [transnational](/wiki/Transnational_marriage "Transnational marriage"), and [same-sex](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage "Same-sex marriage") couples as well as immigrant couples, couples with an immigrant spouse, and other minority couples. In some areas, child marriages and polygamy may occur in spite of national laws against the practice.

#### Marriage license, civil ceremony and registration

Main article: [Wedding](/wiki/Wedding "Wedding")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Shinto_married_couple.jpg/250px-Shinto_married_couple.jpg)](/wiki/File:Shinto_married_couple.jpg)

Couple married in a [Shinto](/wiki/Shinto "Shinto") ceremony in [Takayama, Gifu](/wiki/Takayama,_Gifu "Takayama, Gifu") prefecture

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/Assyrian_wedding%2C_Mechelen.jpg/250px-Assyrian_wedding%2C_Mechelen.jpg)](/wiki/File:Assyrian_wedding,_Mechelen.jpg)

A newly married [Assyrian](/wiki/Assyrian_people "Assyrian people") couple

A marriage is usually formalized at a wedding or marriage ceremony. The ceremony may be officiated either by a religious official, by a government official or by a state approved celebrant. In various European and Latin American countries, any religious ceremony must be held separately from the required civil ceremony. Some countries – such as [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium"), [Bulgaria](/wiki/Bulgaria "Bulgaria"), [France](/wiki/France "France"), the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands"), [Romania](/wiki/Romania "Romania") and [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey")[[141]](#cite_note-145) – require that a civil ceremony take place before any religious one. In some countries – notably the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), [Ireland](/wiki/Republic_of_Ireland "Republic of Ireland"), [Norway](/wiki/Norway "Norway"), and [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain") – both ceremonies can be held together; the officiant at the religious and civil ceremony also serving as agent of the state to perform the civil ceremony. To avoid any implication that the state is "recognizing" a religious marriage (which is prohibited in some countries) – the "civil" ceremony is said to be taking place at the same time as the religious ceremony. Often this involves simply signing a register during the religious ceremony. If the civil element of the religious ceremony is omitted, the marriage ceremony is not recognized as a marriage by government under the law.

Some countries, such as Australia, permit marriages to be held in private and at any location; others, including [England and Wales](/wiki/England_and_Wales "England and Wales"), require that the civil ceremony be conducted in a place open to the public and specially sanctioned by law for the purpose. In England, the place of marriage formerly had to be a church or [register office](/wiki/Register_office_(United_Kingdom) "Register office (United Kingdom)"), but this was extended to any public venue with the necessary licence. An exception can be made in the case of marriage by special emergency license (UK: licence), which is normally granted only when one of the parties is terminally ill. Rules about where and when persons can marry vary from place to place. Some regulations require one of the parties to reside within the jurisdiction of the register office (formerly parish).

Each religious authority has rules for the manner in which marriages are to be conducted by their officials and members. Where religious marriages are recognised by the state, the officiator must also conform with the law of the jurisdiction.

#### Common-law marriage

See also: [Common-law marriage](/wiki/Common-law_marriage "Common-law marriage")

In a small number of jurisdictions marriage relationships may be created by the operation of the law alone.[[142]](#cite_note-Nolo-146) Unlike the typical [ceremonial marriage](/wiki/Ceremonial_marriage "Ceremonial marriage") with legal contract, wedding ceremony, and other details, a [common-law marriage](/wiki/Common-law_marriage "Common-law marriage") may be called "marriage by habit and repute (cohabitation)." A de facto common-law marriage without a license or ceremony is legally binding in some jurisdictions but has no legal consequence in others.[[142]](#cite_note-Nolo-146)

#### Civil unions

Main article: [Civil union](/wiki/Civil_union "Civil union")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/New_York_City_Proposition_8_Protest_outside_LDS_temple_20.jpg/250px-New_York_City_Proposition_8_Protest_outside_LDS_temple_20.jpg)](/wiki/File:New_York_City_Proposition_8_Protest_outside_LDS_temple_20.jpg)

Various advocates of same-sex marriage, such as this protester at a demonstration in New York City against [California Proposition 8](/wiki/California_Proposition_8_(2008) "California Proposition 8 (2008)"), consider [civil unions](/wiki/Civil_unions "Civil unions") an inferior alternative to legal recognition of same-sex marriage.[[143]](#cite_note-Towleroad-147)

A *civil union*, also referred to as a *civil partnership*, is a legally recognized form of partnership similar to marriage. Beginning with [Denmark](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage_in_Denmark "Same-sex marriage in Denmark") in 1989, civil unions under one name or another have been established by law in several countries in order to provide [same-sex couples](/wiki/Homosexuality "Homosexuality") [rights](/wiki/Rights "Rights"), benefits, and [responsibilities](/wiki/Moral_responsibility "Moral responsibility") similar (in some countries, identical) to opposite-sex civil marriage. In some [jurisdictions](/wiki/Jurisdiction "Jurisdiction"), such as [Brazil](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage_in_Brazil "Same-sex marriage in Brazil"), [New Zealand](/wiki/Civil_union_in_New_Zealand "Civil union in New Zealand"), [Uruguay](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage_in_Uruguay "Same-sex marriage in Uruguay"), [Ecuador](/wiki/Recognition_of_same-sex_unions_in_Ecuador "Recognition of same-sex unions in Ecuador"), [France](/wiki/Civil_solidarity_pact "Civil solidarity pact") and the U.S. states of [Hawaii](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage_in_Hawaii "Same-sex marriage in Hawaii") and [Illinois](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage_in_Illinois "Same-sex marriage in Illinois"), civil unions are also open to opposite-sex couples.

#### "Marriage of convenience"

Sometimes people marry to take advantage of a certain situation, sometimes called a [marriage of convenience](/wiki/Marriage_of_convenience "Marriage of convenience") or a sham marriage. In 2003, over 180,000 immigrants were admitted to the U.S. as spouses of [U.S. citizens](/wiki/Citizenship_of_the_United_States "Citizenship of the United States");[[144]](#cite_note-148) more were admitted as fiancés of US citizens for the purpose of being married within 90 days. These marriages had a diverse range of motives, including [obtaining permanent residency](/wiki/Green_card_marriage "Green card marriage"), securing an inheritance that has a marriage clause, or to enroll in health insurance, among many others. While all marriages have a complex combination of conveniences motivating the parties to marry, a marriage of convenience is one that is devoid of normal reasons to marry. In certain countries like Singapore sham marriages are punishable criminal offences.[[145]](#cite_note-149)

### Contemporary legal and human rights criticisms of marriage

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/Esposas_de_Matrimonio_1.jpg/250px-Esposas_de_Matrimonio_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Esposas_de_Matrimonio_1.jpg)

"*Esposas de Matrimonio*" ("Wedding Cuffs"), a [wedding ring](/wiki/Wedding_ring "Wedding ring") sculpture expressing the criticism of marriages' effects on individual liberty. *Esposas* is a play on Spanish, in which the singular form of the word *esposa* refers to a spouse, and the plural refers to [handcuffs](/wiki/Handcuffs "Handcuffs").

Main article: [Criticism of marriage](/wiki/Criticism_of_marriage "Criticism of marriage")

People have proposed arguments against marriage for reasons that include political, philosophical and religious criticisms; concerns about the [divorce rate](/wiki/Divorce_demography "Divorce demography"); individual liberty and gender equality; questioning the necessity of having a personal relationship sanctioned by government or religious authorities; or the promotion of [celibacy](/wiki/Celibacy "Celibacy") for religious or philosophical reasons. Research has found that unhappily married couples are at 3–25 times the risk of developing clinical depression.[[146]](#cite_note-150)[[147]](#cite_note-151)[[148]](#cite_note-152)

#### Power and gender roles

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/Are_married_women_required_by_law_to_obey_their_husbands.%2C_OWID.svg/500px-Are_married_women_required_by_law_to_obey_their_husbands.%2C_OWID.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Are_married_women_required_by_law_to_obey_their_husbands.,_OWID.svg)

Countries where married women are required by law to obey their husbands as of 2023[[149]](#cite_note-153)

Historically, in most cultures, married women had very few rights of their own, being considered, along with the family's children, the property of the [husband](/wiki/Husband "Husband"); as such, they could not own or inherit property, or represent themselves legally (see, for example, [coverture](/wiki/Coverture "Coverture")). Since the late 19th century, in some (primarily [Western](/wiki/Western_world "Western world")) countries, marriage has undergone gradual legal changes, aimed at improving the rights of the wife. These changes included giving wives legal identities of their own, abolishing the right of husbands to physically discipline their wives, giving wives property rights, liberalizing [divorce](/wiki/Divorce "Divorce") laws, providing wives with [reproductive rights](/wiki/Reproductive_rights "Reproductive rights") of their own, and requiring a [wife](/wiki/Wife "Wife")'s consent when sexual relations occur. In the 21st century, there continue to be controversies regarding the legal status of married women, legal acceptance of or leniency towards violence within marriage (especially sexual violence), traditional marriage customs such as [dowry](/wiki/Dowry "Dowry") and [bride price](/wiki/Bride_price "Bride price"), forced marriage, [marriageable age](/wiki/Marriageable_age "Marriageable age"), and criminalization of consensual behaviors such as [premarital](/wiki/Premarital_sex "Premarital sex") and [extramarital sex](/wiki/Extramarital_sex "Extramarital sex").

[Feminist theory](/wiki/Feminist_theory "Feminist theory") approaches opposite-sex marriage as an institution traditionally rooted in [patriarchy](/wiki/Patriarchy "Patriarchy") that promotes male superiority and power over women. This [power dynamic](/wiki/Power_(social_and_political) "Power (social and political)") conceptualizes men as "the provider operating in the public sphere" and women as "the caregivers operating within the private sphere".[[150]](#cite_note-Weadock-154) "Theoretically, women ... [were] defined as the property of their husbands .... The adultery of a woman was always treated with more severity than that of a man."[[151]](#cite_note-155) "[F]eminist demands for a wife's control over her own property were not met [in parts of Britain] until ... [laws were passed in the late 19th century]."[[152]](#cite_note-156)

Traditional heterosexual marriage imposed an obligation of the wife to be sexually available for her husband and an obligation of the husband to provide material/financial support for the wife. Numerous philosophers, feminists and other academic figures have commented on this throughout history, condemning the hypocrisy of legal and religious authorities in regard to sexual issues; pointing to the lack of choice of a woman in regard to controlling her own sexuality; and drawing parallels between marriage, an institution promoted as sacred, and [prostitution](/wiki/Prostitution "Prostitution"), widely condemned and vilified (though often tolerated as a "[necessary evil](/wiki/Lesser_of_two_evils_principle "Lesser of two evils principle")"). [Mary Wollstonecraft](/wiki/Mary_Wollstonecraft "Mary Wollstonecraft"), in the 18th century, described marriage as "legal prostitution".[[153]](#cite_note-157) [Bertrand Russell](/wiki/Bertrand_Russell "Bertrand Russell") in his book *[Marriage and Morals](/wiki/Marriage_and_Morals "Marriage and Morals")* wrote that: "Marriage is for woman the commonest mode of livelihood, and the total amount of undesired sex endured by women is probably greater in marriage than in prostitution."[[154]](#cite_note-158) [Angela Carter](/wiki/Angela_Carter "Angela Carter") in *[Nights at the Circus](/wiki/Nights_at_the_Circus "Nights at the Circus")* wrote: "What is marriage but prostitution to one man instead of many?"[[155]](#cite_note-159)

Some critics object to what they see as [propaganda](/wiki/Propaganda "Propaganda") in relation to marriage – from the government, religious organizations, the media – which aggressively promote marriage as a solution for all social problems; such propaganda includes, for instance, [marriage promotion](/wiki/Marriage_promotion "Marriage promotion") in schools, where children, especially [girls](/wiki/Girl "Girl"), are bombarded with positive information about marriage, being presented only with the information prepared by authorities.[[156]](#cite_note-160)[[157]](#cite_note-161)

The performance of dominant gender roles by men and submissive gender roles by women influence the power dynamic of a heterosexual marriage.[[158]](#cite_note-162) In some American households, women internalize gender role stereotypes and often assimilate into the role of "wife", "mother", and "caretaker" in [conformity](/wiki/Conformity "Conformity") to societal norms and their male partner. Author [bell hooks](/wiki/Bell_hooks "Bell hooks") states "within the family structure, individuals learn to accept sexist oppression as 'natural' and are primed to support other forms of oppression, including heterosexist domination."[[159]](#cite_note-163) "[T]he cultural, economic, political and legal supremacy of the husband" was "[t]raditional ... under English law".[[160]](#cite_note-164) This patriarchal dynamic is contrasted with a conception of [egalitarian](/wiki/Egalitarianism "Egalitarianism") or [peer marriage](/wiki/Shared_earning/shared_parenting_marriage "Shared earning/shared parenting marriage") in which power and labour are divided equally, and not according to [gender roles](/wiki/Gender_role "Gender role").[[150]](#cite_note-Weadock-154)

In the US, studies have shown that, despite egalitarian ideals being common, less than half of respondents viewed their opposite-sex relationships as equal in power, with unequal relationships being more commonly dominated by the male partner.[[161]](#cite_note-Sprecher-165) Studies also show that married couples find the highest level of satisfaction in egalitarian relationships and lowest levels of satisfaction in wife dominate relationships.[[161]](#cite_note-Sprecher-165) In recent years, egalitarian or peer marriages have been receiving increasing focus and attention politically, economically and culturally in a number of countries, including the United States.

#### Extra-marital sex

See also: [Chastity](/wiki/Chastity "Chastity") and [Adultery](/wiki/Adultery "Adultery")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/Jan_Brueghel_the_Elder-Christus_und_die_Ehebrecherin-AP.JPG/250px-Jan_Brueghel_the_Elder-Christus_und_die_Ehebrecherin-AP.JPG)](/wiki/File:Jan_Brueghel_the_Elder-Christus_und_die_Ehebrecherin-AP.JPG)

*[Christ and the woman taken in adultery](/wiki/Jesus_and_the_woman_taken_in_adultery "Jesus and the woman taken in adultery")* by [Jan Brueghel the Elder](/wiki/Jan_Brueghel_the_Elder "Jan Brueghel the Elder"), [Pinakothek](/wiki/Pinakothek "Pinakothek")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d6/Magdalen-asylum.jpg/500px-Magdalen-asylum.jpg)](/wiki/File:Magdalen-asylum.jpg)

[Magdalene laundries](/wiki/Magdalene_laundries "Magdalene laundries") were institutions that existed from the 18th to the late 20th centuries, throughout Europe and North America, where "fallen women", including [unmarried mothers](/wiki/Single_mother "Single mother"), were detained. Photo: Magdalene laundry in [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland "Ireland"), ca. early 20th century.[[162]](#cite_note-166)

Different societies demonstrate variable tolerance of extramarital sex. The Standard Cross-Cultural Sample describes the occurrence of extramarital sex by gender in over 50 pre-industrial cultures.[[163]](#cite_note-Divale,_2000-167)[[164]](#cite_note-Murdock,_White,1969-168) The occurrence of extramarital sex by men is described as "universal" in 6 cultures, "moderate" in 29 cultures, "occasional" in 6 cultures, and "uncommon" in 10 cultures. The occurrence of extramarital sex by women is described as "universal" in 6 cultures, "moderate" in 23 cultures, "occasional" in 9 cultures, and "uncommon" in 15 cultures. Three studies using nationally representative samples in the United States found that between 10 and 15% of women and 20–25% of men engage in extramarital sex.[[165]](#cite_note-Clements,1994-169)[[166]](#cite_note-Laumann,_Gagnon,_Michael,_Michaels,1994-170)[[167]](#cite_note-Wiederman,1997-171)

Many of the world's major religions look with disfavor on [sexual relations](/wiki/Sexual_intercourse "Sexual intercourse") outside marriage.[[168]](#cite_note-172) In some non-[secular](/wiki/Secularity "Secularity") Islamic countries, there are criminal penalties for [sexual intercourse before marriage](/wiki/Fornication "Fornication").[[169]](#cite_note-Kury_Redo_Shea_2016_p._652-173) Sexual relations by a married person with someone other than his/her spouse is known as [adultery](/wiki/Adultery "Adultery"). Adultery is considered in many jurisdictions to be a crime and [grounds for divorce](/wiki/Grounds_for_divorce "Grounds for divorce").

In some countries, such as Saudi Arabia, Pakistan,[[170]](#cite_note-174) Afghanistan,[[171]](#cite_note-175)[[172]](#cite_note-travel.state.gov-176) Iran,[[172]](#cite_note-travel.state.gov-176) Kuwait,[[173]](#cite_note-177) Maldives,[[174]](#cite_note-178) Morocco,[[175]](#cite_note-179) Oman,[[176]](#cite_note-180) Mauritania,[[177]](#cite_note-181) United Arab Emirates,[[178]](#cite_note-182)[[179]](#cite_note-183) Sudan,[[180]](#cite_note-184) Yemen,[[181]](#cite_note-185) any form of sexual activity outside marriage is illegal.

In some parts of the world, women and girls accused of having sexual relations outside marriage are at risk of becoming victims of [honor killings](/wiki/Honor_killing "Honor killing") committed by their families.[[182]](#cite_note-186)[[183]](#cite_note-187) In 2011 several people were sentenced to death by [stoning](/wiki/Stoning "Stoning") after being accused of adultery in Iran, Somalia, Afghanistan, Sudan, Mali and Pakistan.[[184]](#cite_note-188)[[185]](#cite_note-189)[[186]](#cite_note-190)[[187]](#cite_note-191)[[188]](#cite_note-192)[[189]](#cite_note-193)[[190]](#cite_note-194)[[191]](#cite_note-195)[[192]](#cite_note-196) Practices such as honor killings and stoning continue to be supported by mainstream politicians and other officials in some countries. In [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan"), after the 2008 [Balochistan honour killings](/wiki/Honour_killing_in_Pakistan "Honour killing in Pakistan") in which five women were killed by tribesmen of the [Umrani Tribe](/wiki/Umrani "Umrani") of [Balochistan](/wiki/Balochistan,_Pakistan "Balochistan, Pakistan"), Pakistani Federal Minister for Postal Services [Israr Ullah Zehri](/wiki/Israr_Ullah_Zehri "Israr Ullah Zehri") defended the practice.[[193]](#cite_note-times-197)

#### Sexual violence

Main article: [Marital rape](/wiki/Marital_rape "Marital rape")

An issue that is a serious concern regarding marriage and which has been the object of international scrutiny is that of [sexual violence within marriage](/wiki/Marital_rape "Marital rape"). Throughout much of the history, in most cultures, sex in marriage was considered a 'right', that could be taken by force (often by a man from a woman), if 'denied'. As the concept of [human rights](/wiki/Human_rights "Human rights") started to develop in the 20th century, and with the arrival of [second-wave feminism](/wiki/Second-wave_feminism "Second-wave feminism"), such views, and laws, have become less widely held.[[194]](#cite_note-198)

The legal and social concept of marital rape has developed in most industrialized countries in the mid- to late 20th century; in many other parts of the world it is not recognized as a form of abuse, socially or legally. Several countries in [Eastern Europe](/wiki/Eastern_Europe "Eastern Europe") and [Scandinavia](/wiki/Scandinavia "Scandinavia") made marital rape illegal before 1970, and other countries in [Western Europe](/wiki/Western_Europe "Western Europe") and the English-speaking [Western world](/wiki/Western_world "Western world") outlawed it in the 1980s and 1990s. In [England and Wales](/wiki/England_and_Wales "England and Wales"), marital rape was made illegal in 1991. Although marital rape is being increasingly criminalized in [developing countries](/wiki/Developing_country "Developing country") too, cultural, religious, and traditional ideologies about "conjugal rights" remain very strong in many parts of the world; and even where countries may have adequate laws against rape in marriage, these laws may rarely be enforced.[[195]](#cite_note-Banerjee_Rao_2022_pp._11–13-199)

Apart from the issue of rape committed against one's spouse, marriage is, in many parts of the world, closely connected with other forms of sexual violence: in some places, like [Morocco](/wiki/Morocco "Morocco"), unmarried girls and women who are raped are often forced by their families to marry their rapist. Because being the victim of rape and losing [virginity](/wiki/Virginity "Virginity") carry extreme social stigma, and the victims are deemed to have their "reputation" tarnished, a marriage with the rapist is arranged. This is claimed to be in the advantage of both the victim – who does not remain unmarried and does not lose social status – and of the rapist, who avoids punishment. In 2012, after a Moroccan 16-year-old girl committed [suicide](/wiki/Suicide "Suicide") after having been forced by her family to marry her rapist and enduring further abuse by the rapist after they married, there have been protests from activists against this practice which is common in Morocco.[[196]](#cite_note-200)

In some societies, the very high social and religious importance of marital fidelity, especially female fidelity, has as result the criminalization of adultery, often with harsh penalties such as [stoning](/wiki/Stoning "Stoning") or [flogging](/wiki/Flagellation "Flagellation"); as well as leniency towards punishment of violence related to infidelity (such as [honor killings](/wiki/Honor_killing "Honor killing")).[[197]](#cite_note-201) In the 21st century, criminal laws against adultery have become controversial with international organizations calling for their abolition.[[198]](#cite_note-202)[[199]](#cite_note-ohchr.org-203) Opponents of adultery laws argue that these laws are a major contributor to discrimination and violence against women, as they are enforced selectively mostly against women; that they prevent women from reporting [sexual violence](/wiki/Sexual_violence "Sexual violence"); and that they maintain social norms which justify violent crimes committed against women by husbands, families and communities. A Joint Statement by the United Nations Working Group on discrimination against women in law and in practice states that "Adultery as a criminal offence violates women's human rights".[[199]](#cite_note-ohchr.org-203) Some human rights organizations argue that the criminalization of adultery also violates internationally recognized protections for private life, as it represents an arbitrary interference with an individual's privacy, which is not permitted under international law.[[200]](#cite_note-204)

#### Laws, human rights and gender status

The laws surrounding heterosexual marriage in many countries have come under international scrutiny because they contradict international standards of [human rights](/wiki/Human_rights "Human rights"); institutionalize [violence against women](/wiki/Violence_against_women "Violence against women"), [child marriage](/wiki/Child_marriage "Child marriage") and [forced marriage](/wiki/Forced_marriage "Forced marriage"); require the permission of a husband for his wife to work in a paid job, sign legal documents, file criminal charges against someone, sue in civil court etc.; sanction the use by husbands of violence to "discipline" their wives; and discriminate against women in divorce.[[201]](#cite_note-205)[[202]](#cite_note-206)[[203]](#cite_note-207)

Such things were legal even in many Western countries until recently: for instance, in [France](/wiki/France "France"), married women obtained the right to work without their husband's permission in 1965,[[204]](#cite_note-208)[[205]](#cite_note-209)[[206]](#cite_note-210) and in [West Germany](/wiki/West_Germany "West Germany") women obtained this right in 1977 (by comparison women in [East Germany](/wiki/East_Germany "East Germany") had many more rights).[[207]](#cite_note-211)[[208]](#cite_note-212) In [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain"), during Franco's era, a married woman needed her husband's consent, referred to as the *permiso marital*, for almost all economic activities, including employment, ownership of property, and even traveling away from home; the *permiso marital* was abolished in 1975.[[209]](#cite_note-213)

An absolute submission of a wife to her husband is accepted as natural in many parts of the world, for instance surveys by UNICEF have shown that the percentage of women aged 15–49 who think that a husband is justified in hitting or beating his wife under certain circumstances is as high as 90% in Afghanistan and Jordan, 87% in Mali, 86% in Guinea and Timor-Leste, 81% in Laos, 80% in Central African Republic.[[210]](#cite_note-214) Detailed results from Afghanistan show that 78% of women agree with a beating if the wife "goes out without telling him [the husband]" and 76% agree "if she argues with him".[[211]](#cite_note-215)

Throughout history, and still today in many countries, laws have provided for [extenuating circumstances](/wiki/Extenuating_circumstances "Extenuating circumstances"), partial or complete defenses, for men who killed their wives due to adultery, with such acts often being seen as [crimes of passion](/wiki/Crime_of_passion "Crime of passion") and being covered by legal defenses such as [provocation](/wiki/Provocation_(legal) "Provocation (legal)") or defense of [family honor](/wiki/Honor_killing "Honor killing").[[212]](#cite_note-216)

#### Right and ability to divorce

While international law and conventions recognize the need for consent for entering a marriage – namely that people cannot be forced to get married against their will – the right to obtain a divorce is not recognized; therefore holding a person in a marriage against their will (if such person has consented to entering in it) is not considered a violation of human rights, with the issue of divorce being left at the appreciation of individual states.[[213]](#cite_note-217)

In the EU, the last country to allow divorce was [Malta](/wiki/Malta "Malta"), [in 2011](/wiki/Maltese_divorce_referendum,_2011 "Maltese divorce referendum, 2011"). Around the world, the only countries to forbid divorce are [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines") and [Vatican City](/wiki/Vatican_City "Vatican City"),[[214]](#cite_note-218) although in practice in many countries which use a [fault-based divorce](/wiki/Divorce#At-fault_divorce "Divorce") system obtaining a divorce is very difficult. The ability to divorce, in law and practice, has been and continues to be a controversial issue in many countries, and public discourse involves different ideologies such as feminism, [social conservatism](/wiki/Social_conservatism "Social conservatism"), religious interpretations.[[215]](#cite_note-219)

#### Dowry and bridewealth

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/Say_no_to_dowry.jpg/250px-Say_no_to_dowry.jpg)](/wiki/File:Say_no_to_dowry.jpg)

Anti-dowry poster in [Bangalore, India](/wiki/Bangalore "Bangalore")

In recent years, the customs of [dowry](/wiki/Dowry "Dowry") and [bride price](/wiki/Bride_price "Bride price") have received international criticism for inciting conflicts between families and clans; contributing to [violence against women](/wiki/Violence_against_women "Violence against women"); promoting materialism; increasing property crimes (where men steal goods such as cattle in order to be able to pay the bride price); and making it difficult for poor people to marry. African women's rights campaigners advocate the abolishing of bride price, which they argue is based on the idea that women are a form of property which can be bought.[[216]](#cite_note-220) Bride price has also been criticized for contributing to [child trafficking](/wiki/Child_trafficking "Child trafficking") as impoverished parents sell their young daughters to rich older men.[[217]](#cite_note-221) A senior Papua New Guinea police officer has called for the abolishing of bride price arguing that it is one of the main reasons for the mistreatment of women in that country.[[218]](#cite_note-222) The opposite practice of dowry has been linked to a high level of violence (see [Dowry death](/wiki/Dowry_death "Dowry death")) and to crimes such as [extortion](/wiki/Extortion "Extortion").[[219]](#cite_note-223)

#### Children born outside marriage

Further information: [Legitimacy (family law)](/wiki/Legitimacy_(family_law) "Legitimacy (family law)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/The_Outcast_%281851%29_-_Richard_Redgrave.jpg/500px-The_Outcast_%281851%29_-_Richard_Redgrave.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Outcast_(1851)_-_Richard_Redgrave.jpg)

*The Outcast*, by [Richard Redgrave](/wiki/Richard_Redgrave "Richard Redgrave"), 1851. A patriarch casts his daughter and her illegitimate baby out of the family home.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Percentage_of_birth_to_unmarried_women%2C_selected_countries%2C_1980_and_2007.png/500px-Percentage_of_birth_to_unmarried_women%2C_selected_countries%2C_1980_and_2007.png)](/wiki/File:Percentage_of_birth_to_unmarried_women,_selected_countries,_1980_and_2007.png)

Percentage of births to unmarried women, selected countries, 1980 and 2007[[220]](#cite_note-non_mar1-224)

Historically, and still in many countries, children born outside marriage suffered severe social stigma and discrimination. In England and Wales, such children were known as [bastards and whoresons](/wiki/Bastard_(law_of_England_and_Wales) "Bastard (law of England and Wales)").

There are significant differences between world regions in regard to the social and legal position of non-marital births, ranging from being fully accepted and uncontroversial to being severely stigmatized and discriminated.[[221]](#cite_note-225)[[222]](#cite_note-226)

The 1975 European Convention on the Legal Status of Children Born out of Wedlock protects the rights of children born to unmarried parents.[[223]](#cite_note-227) The convention states, among others, that: "The father and mother of a child born out of wedlock shall have the same obligation to maintain the child as if it were born in wedlock" and that "A child born out of wedlock shall have the same right of succession in the estate of its father and its mother and of a member of its father's or mother's family, as if it had been born in wedlock."[[224]](#cite_note-228)

While in most Western countries legal inequalities between children born inside and outside marriage have largely been abolished, this is not the case in some parts of the world.

The legal status of an unmarried father differs greatly from country to country. Without voluntary formal recognition of the child by the father, in most cases there is a need of due process of law in order to establish [paternity](/wiki/Paternity_law "Paternity law"). In some countries however, unmarried cohabitation of a couple for a specific period of time does create a presumption of paternity similar to that of formal marriage. This is the case in Australia.[[225]](#cite_note-229) Under what circumstances can a paternity action be initiated, the rights and responsibilities of a father once paternity has been established (whether he can obtain parental responsibility and whether he can be forced to [support the child](/wiki/Child_support "Child support")) as well as the legal position of a father who voluntarily acknowledges the child, vary widely by jurisdiction. A special situation arises when a married woman has a child by a man other than her husband. Some countries, such as [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel"), refuse to accept a legal challenge of paternity in such a circumstance, in order to avoid the stigmatization of the child (see [Mamzer](/wiki/Mamzer "Mamzer"), a concept under [Jewish law](/wiki/Halakha "Halakha")). In 2010, the [European Court of Human Rights](/wiki/European_Court_of_Human_Rights "European Court of Human Rights") ruled in favor of a German man who had fathered twins with a married woman, granting him right of contact with the twins, despite the fact that the mother and her husband had forbidden him to see the children.[[226]](#cite_note-230)

The steps that an unmarried father must take in order to obtain rights to his child vary by country. In some countries (such as the UK – since 2003 in England and Wales, 2006 in Scotland, and 2002 in Northern Ireland) it is sufficient for the father to be listed on the birth certificate for him to have parental rights;[[227]](#cite_note-231) in other countries, such as Ireland, simply being listed on the birth certificate does not offer any rights, additional legal steps must be taken (if the mother agrees, the parents can both sign a "statutory declaration", but if the mother does not agree, the father has to apply to court).[[228]](#cite_note-232)

Children born outside marriage have become more common, and in some countries, the majority. Recent data from [Latin America](/wiki/Latin_America "Latin America") showed figures for non-marital childbearing to be 74% for [Colombia](/wiki/Colombia "Colombia"), 69% for [Peru](/wiki/Peru "Peru"), 68% for [Chile](/wiki/Chile "Chile"), 66% for [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil"), 58% for [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina "Argentina"), 55% for [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico").[[229]](#cite_note-233)[[230]](#cite_note-234) In 2012, in the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"), 40% of births were outside marriage,[[231]](#cite_note-ec.europa.eu-235) and in the United States, in 2013, the figure was similar, at 41%.[[232]](#cite_note-236) In the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") 48% of births were to unmarried women in 2012; in [Ireland](/wiki/Republic_of_Ireland "Republic of Ireland") the figure was 35%.[[231]](#cite_note-ec.europa.eu-235)

During the first half of the 20th century, unmarried women in some Western countries were coerced by authorities to give their children up for [adoption](/wiki/Adoption "Adoption"). This was especially the case in Australia, through the [forced adoptions in Australia](/wiki/Forced_adoption_in_Australia "Forced adoption in Australia"), with most of these adoptions taking place between the 1950s and the 1970s. In 2013, [Julia Gillard](/wiki/Julia_Gillard "Julia Gillard"), then Prime Minister of Australia, offered a national apology to those affected by the forced adoptions.[[233]](#cite_note-237)[[234]](#cite_note-238)

Some married couples [choose not to have children](/wiki/Voluntary_childlessness "Voluntary childlessness"). Others are unable to have children because of [infertility](/wiki/Infertility "Infertility") or other factors preventing [conception](/wiki/Fertilisation "Fertilisation") or the bearing of children. In some cultures, marriage imposes an *obligation* on women to bear children. In northern [Ghana](/wiki/Ghana "Ghana"), for example, payment of [bridewealth](/wiki/Bride_price "Bride price") signifies a woman's requirement to bear children, and women using birth control face substantial threats of physical abuse and reprisals.[[235]](#cite_note-239)

## Religion

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/Verlobung_Frau_und_Mann_-_mit_Ringtausch_-_und_Treue_Verlobungsversprechen_-_Dresden_-_Bild_011.jpg/250px-Verlobung_Frau_und_Mann_-_mit_Ringtausch_-_und_Treue_Verlobungsversprechen_-_Dresden_-_Bild_011.jpg)](/wiki/File:Verlobung_Frau_und_Mann_-_mit_Ringtausch_-_und_Treue_Verlobungsversprechen_-_Dresden_-_Bild_011.jpg)

A man and woman exchange rings

Further information: [Religion and divorce](/wiki/Religion_and_divorce "Religion and divorce")

Religions develop in specific geographic and social milieux, each with their own constraints as to what constitutes, and who can enter into, a valid religious marriage.[[236]](#cite_note-240) Religious attitudes and practices relating to marriage vary, but have many similarities.[[237]](#cite_note-241)

### Abrahamic religions

#### Baháʼí Faith

The [Baháʼí Faith](/wiki/Bah%C3%A1%CA%BC%C3%AD_Faith "Baháʼí Faith") encourages marriage and views it as a mutually strengthening bond. A [Baháʼí marriage](/wiki/Bah%C3%A1%CA%BC%C3%AD_marriage "Baháʼí marriage") is contingent on the consent of all living parents.[[238]](#cite_note-oneworld-242)

#### Christianity

Main article: [Christian views on marriage](/wiki/Christian_views_on_marriage "Christian views on marriage")

Further information: [Wedding § Christian customs](/wiki/Wedding#Christian_customs "Wedding"), and [Religious views on same-sex marriage](/wiki/Religious_views_on_same-sex_marriage "Religious views on same-sex marriage")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/Crowning_in_Syro-Malabar_Nasrani_Wedding_by_Mar_Gregory_Karotemprel.jpg/250px-Crowning_in_Syro-Malabar_Nasrani_Wedding_by_Mar_Gregory_Karotemprel.jpg)](/wiki/File:Crowning_in_Syro-Malabar_Nasrani_Wedding_by_Mar_Gregory_Karotemprel.jpg)

Crowning during [Holy Matrimony](/wiki/Nasrani_wedding "Nasrani wedding") in the [Syro-Malabar Church](/wiki/Syro-Malabar_Church "Syro-Malabar Church"), an [Eastern Catholic](/wiki/Eastern_Catholic "Eastern Catholic") Church and a part of the [Saint Thomas Christian](/wiki/Saint_Thomas_Christian "Saint Thomas Christian") community in [India](/wiki/India "India")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/St_Mary%27s_Church%2C_Kyoto.jpg/250px-St_Mary%27s_Church%2C_Kyoto.jpg)](/wiki/File:St_Mary%27s_Church,_Kyoto.jpg)

Christian wedding in [Kyoto](/wiki/Kyoto "Kyoto"), Japan

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0f/Russian_church_wedding_in_Toronto%2CCanada.jpg/250px-Russian_church_wedding_in_Toronto%2CCanada.jpg)](/wiki/File:Russian_church_wedding_in_Toronto,Canada.jpg)

[Russian Orthodox](/wiki/Russian_Orthodox "Russian Orthodox") wedding ceremony

Christianity bases its views on marriage upon the teachings of [Jesus](/wiki/Jesus "Jesus") and [Paul the Apostle](/wiki/Paul_the_Apostle "Paul the Apostle").[[239]](#cite_note-Witte-243) Many Christian denominations regard marriage as a [sacrament](/wiki/Sacrament "Sacrament"), sacred institution, or [covenant](/wiki/Covenant_(religion) "Covenant (religion)").[[240]](#cite_note-244) Since the 16th century, five competing models have shaped marriage in the Western tradition, as described by [John Witte Jr.](/wiki/John_Witte_Jr. "John Witte Jr."):[[241]](#cite_note-245)

* Marriage as Sacrament in the Roman Catholic Tradition
* Marriage as Social Estate in the Lutheran Reformation
* Marriage as Covenant in the Reformed (and Methodist) Traditions[[242]](#cite_note-246)
* Marriage as Commonwealth in the Anglican Tradition
* Marriage as Contract in the Enlightenment Tradition

The first known decrees on marriage were during the Catholic [Council of Trent](/wiki/Council_of_Trent "Council of Trent") (1563), decrees that made the validity of marriage dependent on the wedding occurring in the presence of a priest and two witnesses.[[243]](#cite_note-monger-247)[[244]](#cite_note-omalley-248) The absence of a requirement of parental consent ended a debate from the 12th century.[[244]](#cite_note-omalley-248)[[245]](#cite_note-249) In the case of a civil [divorce](/wiki/Divorce "Divorce"), the innocent spouse has no right to marry again until the death of the other spouse terminates the still valid marriage, even if the other spouse was guilty of adultery.[[244]](#cite_note-omalley-248)

The Christian Church performed marriages in the [narthex](/wiki/Narthex "Narthex") of the church prior to the 16th century, when the emphasis was on the marital contract and betrothal. Subsequently, the ceremony moved inside the [sacristy](/wiki/Sacristy "Sacristy") of the church.[[243]](#cite_note-monger-247)[[246]](#cite_note-250)

Catholics, [Eastern Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church"), as well as many [Anglicans](/wiki/Anglicanism "Anglicanism") and [Methodists](/wiki/Methodism "Methodism"), consider marriage termed *holy matrimony* to be an expression of [divine grace](/wiki/Divine_grace "Divine grace"),[[247]](#cite_note-Jr.Warrick2013-251) termed a *[sacrament](/wiki/Sacrament "Sacrament")* and *[mystery](/wiki/Sacred_mysteries "Sacred mysteries")* in the first two Christian traditions. In [Western ritual](/wiki/Latin_liturgical_rites "Latin liturgical rites"), the ministers of the sacrament are the spouses themselves, with a [bishop](/wiki/Bishop "Bishop"), [priest](/wiki/Priest "Priest"), or [deacon](/wiki/Deacon "Deacon") merely witnessing the union on behalf of the Church and blessing it. In [Eastern ritual churches](/wiki/Eastern_Christianity "Eastern Christianity"), the bishop or priest functions as the minister of the Sacred Mystery; Eastern Orthodox deacons may not perform marriages. Western Christians commonly refer to marriage as a [vocation](/wiki/Vocation "Vocation"), while Eastern Christians consider it an [ordination](/wiki/Ordination "Ordination") and a [martyrdom](/wiki/Martyr "Martyr").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Marriage is commonly celebrated in the context of a [Eucharistic](/wiki/Eucharist "Eucharist") service.

The Roman Catholic tradition of the 12th and 13th centuries defined marriage as a sacrament ordained by God,[[239]](#cite_note-Witte-243) signifying the mystical marriage of Christ to his Church.[[248]](#cite_note-252)

> The matrimonial covenant, by which a man and a woman establish between themselves a partnership of the whole of life, is by its nature ordered toward the good of the spouses and the procreation and education of offspring; this covenant between baptized persons has been raised by Christ the Lord to the dignity of a sacrament.[[249]](#cite_note-253)

For Catholic and Methodist Christians, the mutual love between husband and wife becomes an image of the eternal love with which God loves humankind.[[250]](#cite_note-Church2016-254) In the [United Methodist Church](/wiki/United_Methodist_Church "United Methodist Church"), the celebration of Holy Matrimony ideally occurs in the context of a Service of Worship, which includes the celebration of the Eucharist.[[247]](#cite_note-Jr.Warrick2013-251) Likewise, the celebration of marriage between two Catholics normally takes place during celebration of the Holy Mass, because of its sacramental connection with the unity of the Paschal mystery of Christ (Communion). Sacramental marriage confers a perpetual and exclusive bond between spouses. By its nature, the institution of marriage and conjugal love is ordered to the procreation of offspring. Marriage creates rights and duties in the Church between the spouses and towards their children: "[e]ntering marriage with the intention of never having children is a grave wrong and more than likely grounds for an annulment".[[251]](#cite_note-255) According to Roman Catholic legislation, progeny of annulled relationships are considered legitimate. Civilly remarried persons who civilly divorced a living and lawful spouse are not separated from the Church, but cannot receive Eucharistic Communion.[[252]](#cite_note-256)

[Divorce](/wiki/Divorce "Divorce") and [remarriage](/wiki/Remarriage "Remarriage"), while generally not encouraged, are regarded differently by each denomination, with certain traditions, such as the Catholic Church, teaching the concept of an [annulment](/wiki/Annulment "Annulment"). For example, the [Reformed Church in America](/wiki/Reformed_Church_in_America "Reformed Church in America") permits divorce and remarriage,[[253]](#cite_note-RCA1975-257) while connexions such as the [Evangelical Methodist Church Conference](/wiki/Evangelical_Methodist_Church_Conference "Evangelical Methodist Church Conference") forbid divorce except in the case of [fornication](/wiki/Fornication "Fornication") and do not allow for remarriage in any circumstance.[[254]](#cite_note-EMCC2017-258) The [Eastern Orthodox Church](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church") allows divorce for a limited number of reasons, and in theory, but usually not in practice, requires that a marriage after divorce be celebrated with a penitential overtone. With respect to marriage between a Christian and a pagan, the early Church "sometimes took a more lenient view, invoking the so-called Pauline privilege of permissible separation (1 Cor. 7) as legitimate grounds for allowing a convert to divorce a pagan spouse and then marry a Christian."[[255]](#cite_note-259)

The Catholic Church adheres to the proscription of Jesus that married spouses who have consummated their marriage "are no longer two, but one flesh. Therefore, what God has joined together, no human being must separate."[[256]](#cite_note-260) Consequently, the Catholic Church understands that it is wholly without authority to terminate a sacramentally valid and consummated marriage, and its *Codex Iuris Canonici* ([1983 Code of Canon Law](/wiki/1983_Code_of_Canon_Law "1983 Code of Canon Law")) confirms this. Canon 1056 declares that "the essential properties of marriage are unity and *indissolubility*; in [C]hristian marriage they acquire a distinctive *firmness* by reason of the sacrament."[[257]](#cite_note-261) Canon 1057, §2 declares that marriage is "an *irrevocable* covenant".[[258]](#cite_note-262) Therefore, divorce of such a marriage is a metaphysical, moral, and legal impossibility. However, the Church has the authority to annul a presumed "marriage" by declaring it to have been invalid from the beginning, i.e., declaring it never to have been a marriage, in an [annulment](/wiki/Annulment_(Catholic_Church) "Annulment (Catholic Church)") procedure,[[259]](#cite_note-263) which is a fact-finding and fact-declaring effort.

For [Protestant](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism") denominations, the purposes of marriage include intimate companionship, rearing children, and mutual support for spouses to fulfill their callings. Most [Reformed Christians](/wiki/Calvinism "Calvinism") did not regard marriage to the status of a sacrament but considered it a covenant between spouses before God. Some Protestant denominations affirmed that Holy Matrimony is a "[means of grace](/wiki/Means_of_grace "Means of grace"), thus, sacramental in character".[[260]](#cite_note-Mulhall2013-264)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/Phil_and_Marlene.jpg/250px-Phil_and_Marlene.jpg)](/wiki/File:Phil_and_Marlene.jpg)

A couple following their marriage in the [Manti Utah Temple](/wiki/Manti_Utah_Temple "Manti Utah Temple")

Members of [the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints](/wiki/The_Church_of_Jesus_Christ_of_Latter-day_Saints "The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints") (LDS Church) believe "[marriage](/wiki/Marriage_in_The_Church_of_Jesus_Christ_of_Latter-day_Saints "Marriage in The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints") between a man and a woman is ordained of God and that the family is central to the [Creator's plan](/wiki/Plan_of_salvation_(Latter_Day_Saints) "Plan of salvation (Latter Day Saints)") for the eternal destiny of His children."[[261]](#cite_note-265) Their view of marriage is that family relationships can endure beyond the grave.[[262]](#cite_note-266) This is known as 'eternal marriage' which can be eternal only when authorized priesthood holders perform the sealing ordinance in sacred [temples](/wiki/Temple_(LDS_Church) "Temple (LDS Church)").[[263]](#cite_note-267)

Historic Christian belief emphasizes that Christian weddings should occur in a [church](/wiki/Church_(building) "Church (building)") as Christian marriage should begin where one also starts their faith journey; Christians receive the sacrament of [baptism](/wiki/Baptism "Baptism") in church in the presence of their [congregation](/wiki/Church_(congregation) "Church (congregation)").[[264]](#cite_note-Dooley2016-268) Catholic Christian weddings must "take place in a church building" as holy matrimony is a sacrament; sacraments normatively occur in the presence of Christ in the house of God, and "members of the faith community [should be] present to witness the event and provide support and encouragement for those celebrating the sacrament."[[264]](#cite_note-Dooley2016-268) Bishops never grant permission "to those requesting to be married in a garden, on the beach, or some other place outside of the church" and a dispensation is only granted "in extraordinary circumstances (for example, if a bride or groom is ill or disabled and unable to come to the church)."[[264]](#cite_note-Dooley2016-268) Marriage in the church, for Christians, is seen as contributing to the fruit of the newlywed couple regularly attending church each [Lord's Day](/wiki/Lord%27s_Day "Lord's Day") and raising children in the faith.[[264]](#cite_note-Dooley2016-268)

Although many Christian denominations do not perform [same-sex marriages](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage "Same-sex marriage"), many do, such as the [Presbyterian Church (USA)](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_(USA) "Presbyterian Church (USA)"), some dioceses of the [Episcopal Church](/wiki/Episcopal_Church_(United_States) "Episcopal Church (United States)"), the [Metropolitan Community Church](/wiki/Metropolitan_Community_Church "Metropolitan Community Church"), [Quakers](/wiki/Quakers "Quakers"), [United Church of Canada](/wiki/United_Church_of_Canada "United Church of Canada"), and [United Church of Christ](/wiki/United_Church_of_Christ "United Church of Christ") congregations, and some [Anglican](/wiki/Homosexuality_and_the_Anglican_Communion#Anglican_Church_of_Canada "Homosexuality and the Anglican Communion") dioceses.[[265]](#cite_note-269)[[266]](#cite_note-united-270) Same-sex marriage is recognized by various [religious denominations](/wiki/Religious_denomination "Religious denomination").[[267]](#cite_note-pewforum-271)[[268]](#cite_note-NYT_UCC_SSM-272)
