Main article: [Islamic marital jurisprudence](/wiki/Islamic_marital_jurisprudence "Islamic marital jurisprudence")

Pakistani marriage culture video

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/Jeunes_Mari%C3%A9s_dans_le_parc_dAk_Saray_%28Shahrisabz%29_%286018352949%29.jpg/250px-Jeunes_Mari%C3%A9s_dans_le_parc_dAk_Saray_%28Shahrisabz%29_%286018352949%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jeunes_Mari%C3%A9s_dans_le_parc_dAk_Saray_(Shahrisabz)_(6018352949).jpg)

[Newlywed](/wiki/Newlywed "Newlywed") couples visit [Timur](/wiki/Timur "Timur")'s statues to receive wedding blessings in [Uzbekistan](/wiki/Uzbekistan "Uzbekistan").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/Signing_the_Nikah.jpg/250px-Signing_the_Nikah.jpg)](/wiki/File:Signing_the_Nikah.jpg)

A Muslim bride of [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan") origin signing the *nikkah nama* or [marriage certificate](/wiki/Marriage_certificate "Marriage certificate")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c7/Muslim_wedding_in_India.jpg/250px-Muslim_wedding_in_India.jpg)](/wiki/File:Muslim_wedding_in_India.jpg)

A Muslim couple being wed alongside the [Tungabhadra River](/wiki/Tungabhadra_River "Tungabhadra River") at [Hampi](/wiki/Hampi "Hampi"), India

[Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") also commends marriage, with the age of marriage being whenever the individuals feel ready, financially and emotionally.[[269]](#cite_note-273)

In Islam, [polygyny](/wiki/Polygyny "Polygyny") is allowed while [polyandry](/wiki/Polyandry "Polyandry") is not, with the specific [limitation](/wiki/Polygamy_in_Islam "Polygamy in Islam") that a man can have no more than four legal wives at any one time and an unlimited number of [female slaves](/wiki/Islamic_views_on_concubinage "Islamic views on concubinage") as [concubines](/wiki/Concubinage "Concubinage") who may have rights similar wives, with the exception of not being free unless the man has children with them, with the requirement that the man is able and willing to partition his time and wealth equally among the respective wives and concubines (this practice of concubinage, as in Judaism, is not applicable in contemporary times and has been deemed by scholars as invalid due to shifts in views about the role of slavery in the world).[[270]](#cite_note-274)

For a Muslim wedding to take place, the bridegroom and the guardian of the bride (*[wali](/wiki/Wali "Wali")*) must both agree on the marriage. Should the guardian disagree on the marriage, it may not legally take place. If the *wali* of the girl is her father or paternal grandfather, he has the right to force her into marriage even against her proclaimed will, if it is her first marriage, the exercise of this power is, however, very strictly regulated in the interests of the bride. A guardian who is allowed to force the bride into marriage is called *[wali mujbir](/wiki/Marriage_in_Islam#Conditions "Marriage in Islam")*. As minors are not in a position to make a declaration of their wishes which is valid in law, they can only be married at all by a wali mudjbir, but a woman married in this way by another than her ascendant is entitled on coming of age to demand that her marriage be declared void ([faskh](/wiki/Faskh "Faskh")) by the [qadi](/wiki/Qadi "Qadi").[[271]](#cite_note-275)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e4/An_Islamic_wedding_for_a_new_Bride_and_groom.jpg/250px-An_Islamic_wedding_for_a_new_Bride_and_groom.jpg)](/wiki/File:An_Islamic_wedding_for_a_new_Bride_and_groom.jpg)

A [Nigerian](/wiki/Nigerians "Nigerians") Islamic wedding

From an Islamic ([Sharia](/wiki/Sharia "Sharia")) law perspective, the minimum requirements and responsibilities in a Muslim marriage are that the groom provide living expenses to the bride, and in return, the bride's main responsibility is raising children to be proper Muslims. All other rights and responsibilities are to be decided between the husband and wife, and may even be included as stipulations in the marriage contract, so long as they do not go against the minimum requirements of the marriage.

In [Sunni Islam](/wiki/Sunni_Islam "Sunni Islam"), [marriage](/wiki/Islamic_marital_jurisprudence "Islamic marital jurisprudence") must take place in the presence of at least two reliable witnesses, with the consent of the guardian of the bride and the consent of the groom. Following the marriage, the couple may consummate the marriage. To create an '[urf](/wiki/Urf "Urf") marriage, it is sufficient that a man and a woman indicate an intention to marry each other and recite the requisite words in front of a suitable Muslim. The wedding party usually follows but can be held days, or months later, whenever the couple and their families want to; however, there can be no concealment of the marriage as it is regarded as public notification due to the requirement of witnesses.[[272]](#cite_note-276)[[273]](#cite_note-277)[[274]](#cite_note-278)[[275]](#cite_note-279)

In [Shia Islam](/wiki/Shia_Islam "Shia Islam"), marriage may take place without the presence of witnesses as is often the case in temporary [Nikah mut'ah](/wiki/Nikah_mut%27ah "Nikah mut'ah") (prohibited in Sunni Islam), but with the consent of both the bride and the groom. Following the marriage, they may consummate their marriage.[[276]](#cite_note-280)

#### Judaism

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d7/Joodse_bruiloft_Rijksmuseum_SK-A-2598.jpeg/250px-Joodse_bruiloft_Rijksmuseum_SK-A-2598.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Joodse_bruiloft_Rijksmuseum_SK-A-2598.jpeg)

A Jewish wedding, painting by [Jozef Israëls](/wiki/Jozef_Isra%C3%ABls "Jozef Israëls"), 1903

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/77/V03p128a01_Ketubah.jpg/250px-V03p128a01_Ketubah.jpg)](/wiki/File:V03p128a01_Ketubah.jpg)

A [Ketubah](/wiki/Ketubah "Ketubah") in Hebrew, a Jewish marriage-contract outlining the duties of each partner

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/23/Mr_and_Mrs_Hyman_Bauman.jpg/250px-Mr_and_Mrs_Hyman_Bauman.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mr_and_Mrs_Hyman_Bauman.jpg)

Mr. and Mrs. Hyman Bauman, a [Galician Jewish](/wiki/Galician_Jewish "Galician Jewish") couple, as they appeared on their wedding day in 1876 (right) and a half century later in 1926 (left)

Main article: [Jewish views on marriage](/wiki/Jewish_views_on_marriage "Jewish views on marriage")

In [Judaism](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism"), marriage is based on the laws of the [Torah](/wiki/Torah "Torah") and is a contractual bond between spouses in which the spouses agree to be consecrated to one another.[[277]](#cite_note-281) This contract is called [Kiddushin](/wiki/Erusin "Erusin").[[278]](#cite_note-282) Though procreation is not the sole purpose, a Jewish marriage is also expected to fulfill the commandment to have children, as it is written "God blessed them and God said to them, 'Be fertile and increase.'"[[279]](#cite_note-283) The main focus centers around the relationship between the spouses. [Kabbalistically](/wiki/Kabbalah "Kabbalah"), marriage is understood to mean that the spouses are merging into a single soul. In Kabbalistic thought, a man is considered "incomplete" if he is not married, as his soul is only one part of a larger whole that remains to be unified.[[280]](#cite_note-284)

The [Hebrew Bible](/wiki/Hebrew_Bible "Hebrew Bible") describes a number of marriages, including those of [Isaac](/wiki/Isaac "Isaac"),[[281]](#cite_note-285) [Jacob](/wiki/Jacob "Jacob")[[282]](#cite_note-B'reishit_29-286) and [Samson](/wiki/Samson "Samson").[[283]](#cite_note-287) [Polygyny](/wiki/Polygyny "Polygyny"), or men having multiple wives at once, is one of the most common marital arrangements represented in the Hebrew Bible;[[284]](#cite_note-JewEncMar-288) another is that of concubinage ([pilagshut](/wiki/Pilegesh "Pilegesh")) which was often arranged by a man, and a woman who generally enjoyed the same rights as a full legal wife. Other means of concubinage are observed by the author of Judges 19–20,[[285]](#cite_note-289) or during war, as in Deuteronomy 21:10–12.[[286]](#cite_note-290) The rabbis of the Talmud exhibited discomfort with abduction of women in war for the purpose of marriage, declaring it to be a "compromise against man's [evil inclination](/wiki/Yetzer_hara "Yetzer hara")" to be avoided.[[287]](#cite_note-291) Today [Ashkenazi Jews](/wiki/Ashkenazi_Jews "Ashkenazi Jews") are prohibited to take more than one wife because of a ban instituted on this by [Gershom ben Judah](/wiki/Gershom_ben_Judah "Gershom ben Judah") (d. 1040 CE). However, academic scholarship indicates that prohibitions on polygyny may have existed far earlier, based on the [Damascus Covenant](/wiki/Damascus_Document "Damascus Document").[[288]](#cite_note-292)

Among ancient Hebrews, marriage was a domestic affair and not a religious ceremony; no officiant or witness was required by law. [The Rambam](/wiki/Maimonides "Maimonides") wrote that "before the Torah was given, when a man would meet a woman in the marketplace and he and she decided to marry, he would bring her home, conduct relations in private and thus make her his wife." Subsequently, to the Torah being handed down at Sinai, however, the Jews received the commandment that a marriage must be witnessed.[[289]](#cite_note-293)

Betrothal (*[erusin](/wiki/Erusin "Erusin")*), which refers to the time that this binding contract is made, is distinct from marriage itself (*[nissu'in](/wiki/Jewish_wedding "Jewish wedding")*), with the time between these events varying substantially.[[284]](#cite_note-JewEncMar-288)[[290]](#cite_note-CheyneAndBlackMar-294) In biblical times, a wife was regarded as [personal property](/wiki/Personal_property "Personal property"), belonging to her husband;[[284]](#cite_note-JewEncMar-288)[[290]](#cite_note-CheyneAndBlackMar-294) the descriptions of the Bible suggest that she would be expected to perform tasks such as spinning, sewing, weaving, manufacture of clothing, fetching of water, baking of bread, and [animal husbandry](/wiki/Animal_husbandry "Animal husbandry").[[282]](#cite_note-B'reishit_29-286) A husband's obligations to his wife are 1) to provide her with food and care; 2) to supply her clothing and shelter; 3) to share a home with her; 4) to provide the ketubah (marriage contract); 5) to supply medical care if she falls ill; 6) to ransom her back if she is kidnapped; 7) to provide proper burial when she dies; 8) to provide for her materially if he predeceases her; 9) to provide for the support of their daughters until they marry or become adults; and 10) to see that their sons inherit the money specified in her ketubah, in addition to their portion of his estate.[[291]](#cite_note-295) Men are also obligated sexually to their wives, per BT Ketubot 61b:10, with the frequency of marital relations determined in part by the occupation (and hence availability) of the husband.[[292]](#cite_note-296)

Because a wife was regarded as property, her husband was originally free to divorce her for any reason, at any time.[[290]](#cite_note-CheyneAndBlackMar-294) However, Talmudic sources complicate this matter significantly, with Beit Shammai stating that a man may divorce his wife only if she has committed a sexual transgression (such as adultery).[[293]](#cite_note-297) Divorcing a woman against her will was also banned by [Gershom ben Judah](/wiki/Gershom_ben_Judah "Gershom ben Judah") for [Ashkenazi Jews](/wiki/Ashkenazi_Jews "Ashkenazi Jews"). A divorced couple were permitted to remarry, unless the wife had married someone else after her divorce.[[294]](#cite_note-298)

### Hinduism

Main article: [Marriage in Hinduism](/wiki/Marriage_in_Hinduism "Marriage in Hinduism")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/38/Hindu_marriage_ceremony_offering.jpg/250px-Hindu_marriage_ceremony_offering.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hindu_marriage_ceremony_offering.jpg)

Hindu marriage ceremony from a [Rajput](/wiki/Rajput "Rajput") wedding

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/23/Bride_Groom_NP.JPG/250px-Bride_Groom_NP.JPG)](/wiki/File:Bride_Groom_NP.JPG)

A Nepalese Hindu couple in marriage ceremony

[Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism") regards *Vivāha* or *Biye* (marriage) to be a sacred duty that entails both religious and social obligations. It is regarded to be an important [*samskara*](/wiki/Samskara_(rite_of_passage) "Samskara (rite of passage)"), or a rite of passage.[[295]](#cite_note-299) [Hindu texts](/wiki/Hindu_texts "Hindu texts") describe four [*purusharthas*](/wiki/Puru%E1%B9%A3%C4%81rtha "Puruṣārtha") (goals of existence): *[dharma](/wiki/Dharma "Dharma")* (righteousness), *[artha](/wiki/Artha "Artha")* (wealth), *[kama](/wiki/Kama "Kama")* (desire), and *[moksha](/wiki/Moksha "Moksha")* (liberation). The purpose of the marriage *samskara* is to fulfill the goal of *kama*, allowing an adherent to gradually advance towards the attainment of *moksha*. The [Manusmriti](/wiki/Manusmriti "Manusmriti") describes many different types of marriages and their categorisation, ranging from the [*gandharva vivaha*](/wiki/Gandharva_marriage "Gandharva marriage")[[296]](#cite_note-Banerjee_86-300) (a consensual marriage of love between a man and a woman without the performance of rituals or witnesses) to the [*rakshasa vivaha*](/wiki/Rakshasa_marriage "Rakshasa marriage")[[296]](#cite_note-Banerjee_86-300) (a "demoniac" marriage, performed by abduction of one participant by the other participant, usually, but not always, with the help of other persons). In the [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent"), [arranged marriages](/wiki/Arranged_marriage_in_the_Indian_subcontinent "Arranged marriage in the Indian subcontinent"), in which the spouse's parents or an older family member choose the partner, are still predominant in comparison with [love marriages](/wiki/Love_marriage "Love marriage") in the contemporary period. The [Hindu Widow's Remarriage Act 1856](/wiki/Hindu_Widows%27_Remarriage_Act,_1856 "Hindu Widows' Remarriage Act, 1856") empowers a Hindu widow to remarry.[[297]](#cite_note-301)

### Buddhism

Main article: [Buddhist view of marriage](/wiki/Buddhist_view_of_marriage "Buddhist view of marriage")

The Buddhist view of marriage considers marriage a secular affair and thus not a [sacrament](/wiki/Sacrament "Sacrament"). Buddhists are expected to follow the civil laws regarding marriage laid out by their respective governments. Gautama Buddha, being a [kshatriya](/wiki/Kshatriya "Kshatriya") was required by Shakyan tradition to pass a series of tests to prove himself as a warrior, before he was allowed to marry.

### Sikhism

In a Sikh marriage, the couple walks around the *[Guru Granth Sahib](/wiki/Guru_Granth_Sahib "Guru Granth Sahib")* holy book four times, and a holy man recites from it in the [kirtan](/wiki/Kirtan "Kirtan") style. The ceremony is known as '[Anand Karaj](/wiki/Anand_Karaj "Anand Karaj")' and represents the holy union of two souls united as one.

### Wicca

Wiccan marriages are commonly known as handfastings and are a celebration held by Wiccans. [Handfasting](/wiki/Handfasting "Handfasting") was originally a medieval ritual and has been revived by contemporary Pagans. In the ritual, the couple's wrists are tied together to symbolize the binding of two lives. It is commonly used in Wicca and Pagan ceremonies, but it has become more mainstream and comes up in both religious and secular vows and readings. Although handfastings vary for each Wiccan they often involve honoring Wiccan deities.[[298]](#cite_note-Bedard-302) Some Wiccan traditions have a marriage vow "for as long as love lasts" instead of the traditional Christian "till death do us part". The first Wiccan wedding took place in 1960, between [Frederic Lamond](/wiki/Frederic_Lamond_(Wiccan) "Frederic Lamond (Wiccan)") and his wife, Gillian. Most Wiccan traditions will celebrate same-sex and different-sex handfastings.[[298]](#cite_note-Bedard-302) The length of commitment varies from a year and a day (after which the vows may be renewed), "as long as love shall last", for a lifetime, or for future incarnations.

Consensual sex is considered sacred for Wiccans. Some traditions perform the Great Rite, in which a High Priest and High Priestess invoke the God and Goddess on each other before making love. It can be used to raise magical energy for the use of spell work. It can also be performed symbolically, using the athame to symbolize masculine energy and the chalice to symbolize feminine energy.[[299]](#cite_note-303)

## Health

Main article: [Marriage and health](/wiki/Marriage_and_health "Marriage and health")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/Nepalese_marriage.jpg/250px-Nepalese_marriage.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nepalese_marriage.jpg)

Nepali wedding

Marriage, like other close relationships, exerts considerable influence on [health](/wiki/Health "Health").[[300]](#cite_note-Robles2013-304) Married people experience lower [morbidity](/wiki/Morbidity "Morbidity") and mortality across such diverse health threats as [cancer](/wiki/Cancer "Cancer"), [heart attacks](/wiki/Myocardial_infarction "Myocardial infarction"), and [surgery](/wiki/Surgery "Surgery").[[301]](#cite_note-KiecoltGlaser2001-305) Research on marriage and health is part of the broader study of the benefits of social relationships.

Social ties provide people with a sense of identity, purpose, belonging, and support.[[302]](#cite_note-306) Simply being married, as well as the quality of one's marriage, have been linked to diverse measures of health.[[300]](#cite_note-Robles2013-304)[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*]

The health-protective effect of marriage is stronger for men than women.[[301]](#cite_note-KiecoltGlaser2001-305)[[303]](#cite_note-wanic2011-307) Marital status—the simple fact of being married—confers more health benefits to men than women.[[301]](#cite_note-KiecoltGlaser2001-305)

Women's health is more strongly impacted than men's by marital conflict or satisfaction, such that unhappily married women do not enjoy better health relative to their single counterparts.[[301]](#cite_note-KiecoltGlaser2001-305)[[303]](#cite_note-wanic2011-307)[[304]](#cite_note-saxbe2008-308) Most research on marriage and health has focused on heterosexual couples; more work is needed to clarify the health impacts of [same-sex marriage](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage "Same-sex marriage").[[300]](#cite_note-Robles2013-304)

## Divorce and annulment

Main articles: [Divorce](/wiki/Divorce "Divorce") and [Divorce law by country](/wiki/Divorce_law_by_country "Divorce law by country")

In most societies, the death of one of the partners terminates the marriage, and in monogamous societies, this allows the other partner to remarry, though sometimes after a waiting or mourning period.

In some societies, a marriage can be [annulled](/wiki/Annulment "Annulment"), when an authority declares that a marriage never happened. Jurisdictions often have provisions for [void marriages](/wiki/Void_marriage "Void marriage") or [voidable marriages](/wiki/Voidable_marriage "Voidable marriage").

A marriage may also be terminated through [divorce](/wiki/Divorce "Divorce"). Countries that have relatively recently legalized divorce are Italy (1970), Portugal (1975), Brazil (1977), Spain (1981), Argentina (1987), Paraguay (1991), Colombia (1991), Ireland (1996), Chile (2004) and Malta (2011). The [Philippines](/wiki/Divorce_in_the_Philippines "Divorce in the Philippines") and the [Vatican City](/wiki/Vatican_City "Vatican City") are the only jurisdictions which do not allow divorce.[[305]](#cite_note-309) After divorce, one spouse may have to pay [alimony](/wiki/Alimony "Alimony"). [Laws concerning divorce](/wiki/Divorce_law_by_country "Divorce law by country") and the ease with which a divorce can be obtained vary widely around the world. After a divorce or an annulment, the people concerned are free to remarry (or marry).

A statutory right of two married partners to mutually consent to divorce was enacted in western nations in the mid-20th century. In the United States, [no-fault divorce](/wiki/No-fault_divorce "No-fault divorce") was first enacted in California in 1969 and the final state to legalize it was New York in 1989.[[306]](#cite_note-310)

About 45% of marriages in Britain[[307]](#cite_note-311) and, according to a 2009 study, 46% of marriages in the U.S.[[308]](#cite_note-312) end in divorce.

## History

See also: [History of marriage](/wiki/History_of_marriage "History of marriage")

### Ancient world

#### Ancient Near East

Many cultures have legends concerning the origins of marriage. The way in which a marriage is conducted and its rules and ramifications have changed over time, as has the institution itself, depending on the culture or demographic of the time.[[309]](#cite_note-313)

The first recorded evidence of marriage ceremonies uniting a man and a woman dates back to approximately 2350 BC, in ancient [Mesopotamia](/wiki/Mesopotamia "Mesopotamia").[[310]](#cite_note-314) Wedding ceremonies, as well as dowry and divorce, can be traced back to Mesopotamia and [Babylonia](/wiki/Babylonia "Babylonia").[[311]](#cite_note-315)

According to ancient Hebrew tradition, a wife was seen as being property of high value and was, therefore, usually, carefully looked after.[[284]](#cite_note-JewEncMar-288)[[290]](#cite_note-CheyneAndBlackMar-294) Early nomadic communities in the Middle East practiced a form of marriage known as *[beena](/wiki/Beena_marriage "Beena marriage")*, in which a wife would own a tent of her own, within which she retains complete independence from her husband.[[312]](#cite_note-WRSKinship167-316) The [Covenant Code](/wiki/Covenant_Code "Covenant Code") orders "If he take him another; her food, her clothing, and her duty of marriage, shall he not diminish (or lessen)". The [Talmud](/wiki/Talmud "Talmud") interprets this as a requirement for a man to provide food and clothing to, and have sex with, each of his wives.[[313]](#cite_note-JewEncHusWif-317)[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] However, "duty of marriage" is also interpreted as whatever one does as a married couple, which is more than just sexual activity. And the term diminish, which means to lessen, shows the man must treat her as if he was not married to another.

As a [polygynous](/wiki/Polygyny "Polygyny") society, the Israelites did not have any laws that imposed marital fidelity on men.[[314]](#cite_note-CheyneAndBlackJeal-318)[[315]](#cite_note-JewEncAdu-319) [Adulterous](/wiki/Adultery "Adultery") married women, adulterous betrothed women, and the men who slept with them, however, were subject to the [death penalty](/wiki/Capital_punishment "Capital punishment") by [the biblical laws against adultery](/wiki/Adultery#In_the_Hebrew_Bible "Adultery"). The [literary prophets](/wiki/Literary_prophets "Literary prophets") indicate that adultery was a frequent occurrence, despite these legal restrictions.[[314]](#cite_note-CheyneAndBlackJeal-318)

#### Classical Greece and Rome

See also: [Marriage in ancient Rome](/wiki/Marriage_in_ancient_Rome "Marriage in ancient Rome") and [Ancient Greek wedding customs](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_wedding_customs "Ancient Greek wedding customs")

In [ancient Greece](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece"), no specific civil ceremony was required for the creation of a heterosexual marriage – only mutual agreement and the fact that the couple must regard each other as husband and wife accordingly.[[316]](#cite_note-WILLIAMSON_1998-320) Men usually married when they were in their 20s and women in their teens.[[317]](#cite_note-321) It has been suggested that these ages made sense for the Greeks because men were generally done with military service or financially established by their late 20s, and marrying a teenage girl ensured ample time for her to bear children, as life expectancies were significantly lower.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Married Greek women had few rights in ancient Greek society and were expected to take care of the house and children.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Time was an important factor in Greek marriage. For example, there were superstitions that being married during a [full moon](/wiki/Full_moon "Full moon") was good luck and Greeks married in the winter in honor of Hera.[[316]](#cite_note-WILLIAMSON_1998-320) Inheritance was more important than feelings: a woman whose father dies without male heirs could be forced to marry her nearest male relative – even if she had to divorce her husband first.[[318]](#cite_note-PT-322)

There were several types of marriages in ancient Roman society. The traditional ("conventional") form called *conventio in manum* required a ceremony with witnesses and was also dissolved with a ceremony.[[319]](#cite_note-magnusHirschfeldSexology-323) In this type of marriage, a woman lost her family rights of inheritance of her old family and gained them with her new one. She now was subject to the authority of her husband.[[320]](#cite_note-324) There was the free marriage known as *sine manu*. In this arrangement, the wife remained a member of her original family; she stayed under the authority of her father, kept her family rights of inheritance with her old family and did not gain any with the new family.[[321]](#cite_note-RomanEmpireMarriage-325) The minimum age of marriage for girls was 12.[[322]](#cite_note-326)

#### Germanic tribes

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/Seuso_and_his_wife_at_Lake_Balaton.jpg/250px-Seuso_and_his_wife_at_Lake_Balaton.jpg)](/wiki/File:Seuso_and_his_wife_at_Lake_Balaton.jpg)

Seuso and his wife

Among ancient [Germanic](/wiki/Germanic_peoples "Germanic peoples") tribes, the bride and groom were roughly the same age and generally older than their Roman counterparts, at least according to [Tacitus](/wiki/Tacitus "Tacitus"):

> The youths partake late of the pleasures of love, and hence pass the age of puberty unexhausted: nor are the virgins hurried into marriage; the same maturity, the same full growth is required: the sexes unite equally matched and robust, and the children inherit the vigor of their parents.[[323]](#cite_note-327)

Where [Aristotle](/wiki/Aristotle "Aristotle") had set the prime of life at 37 years for men and 18 for women, the [Visigothic Code of law](/wiki/Visigothic_Code "Visigothic Code") in the 7th century placed the prime of life at 20 years for both men and women, after which both presumably married. Tacitus states that ancient Germanic brides were on average about 20 and were roughly the same age as their husbands.[[324]](#cite_note-328) Tacitus, however, had never visited the German-speaking lands and most of his information on [Germania](/wiki/Germania "Germania") comes from secondary sources. In addition, [Anglo-Saxon](/wiki/Anglo-Saxons "Anglo-Saxons") women, like those of other Germanic tribes, are marked as women from the age of 12 and older, based on archaeological finds, implying that the age of marriage coincided with [puberty](/wiki/Puberty "Puberty").[[325]](#cite_note-329)

### Europe

Further information: [History of the family](/wiki/History_of_the_family "History of the family") and [Royal intermarriage](/wiki/Royal_intermarriage "Royal intermarriage")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/Brauysegen_im_Bett.gif/250px-Brauysegen_im_Bett.gif)](/wiki/File:Brauysegen_im_Bett.gif)

Woodcut. How Reymont and Melusina were betrothed / And by the bishop were blessed in their bed on their wedlock. From the *[Melusine](/wiki/Melusine "Melusine")*, 15th century.

From the [early Christian](/wiki/Early_Christianity "Early Christianity") era (30 to 325 CE), marriage was thought of as primarily a private matter, with no uniform religious or other ceremony being required.[[326]](#cite_note-330) However, bishop [Ignatius of Antioch](/wiki/Ignatius_of_Antioch "Ignatius of Antioch"), writing around 110, exhorts, "[I]t becomes both men and women who marry, to form their union with the approval of the bishop, that their marriage may be according to God, and not after their own lust."[[327]](#cite_note-331)

In 12th-century Europe, women took the surname of their husbands and starting in the second half of the 16th century parental consent along with the church's consent was required for marriage.[[328]](#cite_note-those_terrible_middle_ages-332)

With few local exceptions, until 1545, Christian marriages in Europe were by mutual consent, declaration of intention to marry and upon the subsequent physical union of the parties.[[329]](#cite_note-upennExcerptFromBook-333)[[330]](#cite_note-marriageDotAbout-334) The couple would promise verbally to each other that they would be married to each other; the presence of a priest or witnesses was not required.[[331]](#cite_note-335) This promise was known as the "verbum." If freely given and made in the present tense (e.g., "I marry you"), it was unquestionably binding;[[329]](#cite_note-upennExcerptFromBook-333) if made in the future tense ("I will marry you"), it would constitute a [betrothal](/wiki/Engagement "Engagement").

One of the functions of churches from the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages") was to register marriages, which was not obligatory. There was no state involvement in marriage and personal status, with these issues being adjudicated in [ecclesiastical courts](/wiki/Ecclesiastical_court "Ecclesiastical court"). During the Middle Ages marriages were arranged, sometimes as early as birth, and these early pledges to marry were often used to ensure treaties between different royal families, nobles, and heirs of fiefdoms. The church resisted these imposed unions, and increased the number of causes for nullification of these arrangements.[[328]](#cite_note-those_terrible_middle_ages-332) As Christianity spread during the Roman period and the Middle Ages, the idea of free choice in selecting marriage partners increased and spread with it.[[328]](#cite_note-those_terrible_middle_ages-332) In 1563 the [Council of Trent](/wiki/Council_of_Trent "Council of Trent"), twenty-fourth session, required that a valid marriage must be performed by a priest before two witnesses.[[332]](#cite_note-Matrimonios_a_lo_Navarro-336)

In medieval Western Europe, later marriage and higher rates of definitive [celibacy](/wiki/Celibacy "Celibacy") (the so-called "European marriage pattern") helped to constrain patriarchy at its most extreme level. For example, [Medieval England](/wiki/England_in_the_Middle_Ages "England in the Middle Ages") saw marriage age as variable depending on economic circumstances, with couples delaying marriage until the early twenties when times were bad and falling to the late teens after the [Black Death](/wiki/Black_Death "Black Death"), when there were labor shortages;[[333]](#cite_note-337) by appearances, marriage of adolescents was not the norm in England.[[334]](#cite_note-338)[[335]](#cite_note-339) Where the strong influence of classical [Celtic](/wiki/Celts "Celts") and [Germanic](/wiki/Germanic_peoples "Germanic peoples") cultures (which were not rigidly patriarchal)[[336]](#cite_note-340)[[337]](#cite_note-341) helped to offset the Judaeo-Roman patriarchal influence,[[338]](#cite_note-342) in Eastern Europe the tradition of early and universal marriage (often in early [adolescence](/wiki/Adolescence "Adolescence")),[[339]](#cite_note-343) as well as traditional [Slavic](/wiki/Slavs "Slavs") patrilocal custom,[[340]](#cite_note-344) led to a greatly inferior status of women at all levels of society.[[341]](#cite_note-345)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/Dr%C3%A4kt%2C_Gustav_III%2C_gnr_3485_%26_dr%C3%A4kt%2C_Sofia_magdalena%2C_gnr_3502._Fel_f%C3%B6rem%C3%A5lsref_-_Livrustkammaren_-_16527.tif/lossy-page1-250px-Dr%C3%A4kt%2C_Gustav_III%2C_gnr_3485_%26_dr%C3%A4kt%2C_Sofia_magdalena%2C_gnr_3502._Fel_f%C3%B6rem%C3%A5lsref_-_Livrustkammaren_-_16527.tif.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dr%C3%A4kt,_Gustav_III,_gnr_3485_%26_dr%C3%A4kt,_Sofia_magdalena,_gnr_3502._Fel_f%C3%B6rem%C3%A5lsref_-_Livrustkammaren_-_16527.tif)

Swedish royal wedding clothes from 1766 at [Livrustkammaren](/wiki/Livrustkammaren "Livrustkammaren") in Stockholm

The average age of marriage for most of [Northwestern Europe](/wiki/Western_European_marriage_pattern "Western European marriage pattern") from 1500 to 1800 was around [25 years of age](/wiki/Hajnal_line "Hajnal line");[[342]](#cite_note-346)[[343]](#cite_note-347)[[344]](#cite_note-autogenerated82-348) as the Church dictated that both parties had to be at least 21 years of age to marry without the consent of their parents, the bride and groom were roughly the same age, with most brides in their early twenties and most grooms two or three years older,[[344]](#cite_note-autogenerated82-348) and a substantial number of women married for the first time in their thirties and forties, particularly in urban areas,[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3) with the average age at first marriage rising and falling as circumstances dictated. In better times, more people could afford to marry earlier and thus fertility rose and conversely marriages were delayed or forgone when times were bad, thus restricting family size;[[345]](#cite_note-349) after the [Black Death](/wiki/Black_Death "Black Death"), the greater availability of profitable jobs allowed more people to marry young and have more children,[[346]](#cite_note-350) but the stabilization of the population in the 16th century meant fewer job opportunities and thus more people delaying marriages.[[347]](#cite_note-351)

The age of marriage was not absolute, however, as child marriages occurred throughout the Middle Ages and later, with just some of them including:

* The 1552 CE marriage between John Somerford and Jane Somerford Brereto, at the ages of 3 and 2, respectively.[[348]](#cite_note-woman-compendium-352)
* In the early 1900s, [Magnus Hirschfeld](/wiki/Magnus_Hirschfeld "Magnus Hirschfeld") surveyed the age of consent in about 50 countries, which he found to often range between 12 and 16. In the [Vatican](/wiki/Vatican_City "Vatican City"), the age of consent was 12.[[349]](#cite_note-ascrim-persp-353)

As part of the [Protestant Reformation](/wiki/Reformation "Reformation"), the role of recording marriages and setting the rules for marriage passed to the state, reflecting [Martin Luther](/wiki/Martin_Luther "Martin Luther")'s view that marriage was a "worldly thing".[[350]](#cite_note-354) By the 17th century, many of the [Protestant](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism") European countries had a state involvement in marriage.

In England, under the Anglican Church, marriage by consent and cohabitation was valid until the passage of [Marriage Act 1753](/wiki/Marriage_Act_1753 "Marriage Act 1753"). This act instituted certain requirements for marriage, including the performance of a religious ceremony observed by witnesses.[[351]](#cite_note-WestLaw-355)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/Paolo_Monti_-_Serie_fotografica_-_BEIC_6363689.jpg/250px-Paolo_Monti_-_Serie_fotografica_-_BEIC_6363689.jpg)](/wiki/File:Paolo_Monti_-_Serie_fotografica_-_BEIC_6363689.jpg)

A marriage in 1960 in Italy. Photo by [Paolo Monti](/wiki/Paolo_Monti "Paolo Monti").

As part of the [Counter-Reformation](/wiki/Counter-Reformation "Counter-Reformation"), in 1563 the [Council of Trent](/wiki/Council_of_Trent "Council of Trent") decreed that a [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") marriage would be recognized only if the marriage ceremony was officiated by a priest with two witnesses. The council also authorized a [Catechism](/wiki/Catechism "Catechism"), issued in 1566, which defined marriage as "The conjugal union of man and woman, contracted between two qualified persons, which obliges them to live together throughout life."[[239]](#cite_note-Witte-243)

In the [early modern period](/wiki/Early_modern_period "Early modern period"), [John Calvin](/wiki/John_Calvin "John Calvin") and his [Protestant](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism") colleagues reformulated Christian marriage by enacting the Marriage Ordinance of Geneva, which imposed "The dual requirements of state registration and church consecration to constitute marriage"[[239]](#cite_note-Witte-243) for recognition.

In [England and Wales](/wiki/England_and_Wales "England and Wales"), Lord Hardwicke's [Marriage Act 1753](/wiki/Marriage_Act_1753 "Marriage Act 1753") required a formal ceremony of marriage, thereby curtailing the practice of [Fleet Marriage](/wiki/Fleet_Marriage "Fleet Marriage"), an irregular or a clandestine marriage.[[352]](#cite_note-356) From the 1690s until the [Marriage Act 1753](/wiki/Marriage_Act_1753 "Marriage Act 1753") as many as 300,000 clandestine marriages were performed at Fleet Prison alone.[[353]](#cite_note-357) The act required a marriage ceremony to be officiated by an Anglican priest in the [Anglican Church](/wiki/Anglican_Church "Anglican Church") with two witnesses and registration. The act did not apply to Jewish marriages or those of Quakers, whose marriages continued to be governed by their own customs.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/10/Flodmark-Ehrs_Marriage_2016_crop.jpg/250px-Flodmark-Ehrs_Marriage_2016_crop.jpg)](/wiki/File:Flodmark-Ehrs_Marriage_2016_crop.jpg)

Newlyweds after a civil ceremony in the tower of [Stockholm City Hall](/wiki/Stockholm_City_Hall "Stockholm City Hall") in 2016

In England and Wales, since 1837, civil marriages have been recognized as a legal alternative to church marriages under the [Marriage Act 1836](/wiki/Marriage_Act_1836 "Marriage Act 1836"). In Germany, civil marriages were recognized in 1875. This law permitted a declaration of the marriage before an official clerk of the civil administration, when both spouses affirm their will to marry, to constitute a legally recognized valid and effective marriage, and allowed an optional private clerical marriage ceremony.

In contemporary [English common law](/wiki/English_law "English law"), a marriage is a voluntary [contract](/wiki/Contract "Contract") by a man and a woman, in which by agreement they choose to become husband and wife.[[354]](#cite_note-358) Edvard Westermarck proposed that "the institution of marriage has probably developed out of a primeval habit".[[355]](#cite_note-359)

Since the late twentieth century, major social changes in Western countries have led to changes in the demographics of marriage, with the age of first marriage increasing, fewer people marrying, and more couples choosing to [cohabit](/wiki/Cohabitation "Cohabitation") rather than marry. For example, the number of marriages in Europe decreased by 30% from 1975 to 2005.[[356]](#cite_note-360) As of 2000, the average marriage age range was 25–44 years for men and 22–39 years for women.

### China

Main article: [Chinese marriage](/wiki/Chinese_marriage "Chinese marriage")

The mythological origin of Chinese marriage is a story about [Nüwa](/wiki/N%C3%BCwa "Nüwa") and [Fu Xi](/wiki/Fu_Xi "Fu Xi") who invented proper marriage procedures after becoming married. In ancient Chinese society, people of the same surname are supposed to consult with their [family trees](/wiki/Family_tree "Family tree") prior to marriage to reduce the potential risk of unintentional incest. Marrying one's maternal relatives was generally not thought of as incest. Families sometimes intermarried from one generation to another. Over time, Chinese people became more geographically mobile. Individuals remained members of their biological families. When a couple died, the husband and the wife were buried separately in the respective clan's graveyard. In a maternal marriage, a male would become a son-in-law who lived in the wife's home.

The [New Marriage Law](/wiki/New_Marriage_Law "New Marriage Law") of 1950 radically changed Chinese marriage traditions, enforcing [monogamy](/wiki/Monogamy "Monogamy"), equality of men and women, and choice in marriage; [arranged marriages](/wiki/Arranged_marriage "Arranged marriage") were the most common type of marriage in China until then. Starting October 2003, it became legal to marry or divorce without authorization from the couple's work units.[[357]](#cite_note-361)[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] Although people with infectious diseases such as AIDS may now marry, marriage is still illegal for the mentally ill.[[358]](#cite_note-362)

### Korea

The practice of [matrilocality](/wiki/Matrilocality "Matrilocality") in Korea started in the [Goguryeo](/wiki/Goguryeo "Goguryeo") period and ended in the early [Joseon](/wiki/Joseon "Joseon") period.[[359]](#cite_note-363)[[360]](#cite_note-364) The Korean saying that when a man gets married, he is "entering *jangga*" (the house of his father-in-law), stems from the Goguryeo period.[[361]](#cite_note-365)

4. **[^](#cite_ref-usa_135-0)** Same-sex marriage is performed and recognized by law in all [fifty states](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage_law_in_the_United_States_by_state "Same-sex marriage law in the United States by state") and the [District of Columbia](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage_in_the_District_of_Columbia "Same-sex marriage in the District of Columbia"), all territories except [American Samoa](/wiki/LGBT_rights_in_American_Samoa "LGBT rights in American Samoa"), and in [some tribal nations](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage_in_tribal_nations_in_the_United_States "Same-sex marriage in tribal nations in the United States").

"The Qurʾānic word *zinā* (elsewhere more commonly *zināʾ*) means sexual intercourse outside the institutions of marriage and concubinage. Q 17:32 characterizes this behavior as a fāḥisha, i.e. an obscene act of transgression against God from which a Muslim should refrain (cf. Q 25:68). [...] The Ḥanafite jurist al-Jaṣṣāṣ (d. 370/981) explains that adultery and fornication are transgressions because of the social chaos they create. The patrilineal descent of the offspring of such unions is unidentified. Thus, his right to inherit from the father is denied and he cannot know his patrilineal maḥārim, i.e. the relatives with whom sexual intercourse is considered incest."

"The Ḳurʾān disapproved of the promiscuity prevailing at that time in Arabia and forbade e.g. the prostitution of slave girls by their masters (XXIV, 33). Several verses refer to unlawful sexual intercourse. Some of these mention that it is a sin (*fāḥis̲h̲a*) and that it will be punished in the Hereafter (XVII, 32, XXV, 68-9). Most verses, however, deal with the legal aspects. Making *zinā* a punishable offence was a means to enforce the uniform system of marriage introduced by Muḥammad. IV, 15-16 lay down that both parties to it must be punished and required four witnesses for proving the offence."

254. **[^](#cite_ref-EMCC2017_258-0)** *Evangelical Methodist Church Discipline*. [Evangelical Methodist Church Conference](/wiki/Evangelical_Methodist_Church_Conference "Evangelical Methodist Church Conference"). 15 July 2017. pp. 22–21. "The marriage contract is so sacred that we advise against seeking divorce on any grounds whatseover. Should any member seek divorce on any unscriptural grounds (Matt. 5:32 "But I say unto you, that whosoever shall put away his wife, saving for the cause of fornication, causeth her to commit adultery; and whosoever shall marry her that is divorced comitteth adultery."), and that well proven, he shall be summoned to appear at a meeting in the local church, with the general board working in co-operation with the local church board. If proven guilty of such offense, he shall be dismissed at once, and no longer considered a member of Evangelical Methodist Church. We advise against the remarriage of all divorced persons, as the scriptures declare in Romans 7:3a "...So then if, while her husband liveth, she be married to another man, she shall be called an adulteress." If any person becoming converted, and having such marital complications as mentioned above in the days of their sin and ignorance, it is our belief that God will and does forgive them; however, we shall not receive such persons into church membership, but with to extend to them the right hand of fellowship, promising the prayers of God's people. Should any pastor, knowingly or unknowingly, receive such persons that have been divorced and remarried into membership, such membership shall not be valid. Ministers are advised to have nothing to do with the re-marriage of persons divorced on any grounds. In the event any person is divorced by an unbelieving companion and shall remain in an unmarried state, retaining his or her Christian integrity, he or she shall not be dismissed or barred from church membership."
